issue_title: Can this be trained for political groups/committees?
issue_body:
It may be that this is not quite the right tool for us, but I wanted to check.

I have a list of ~60,000,000 names from a campaign financing and lobbying activity database from the state of California. There are many, many duplicates, but there are also many malformed rows. The names are supposed to be divided up into "name-last", 'name-first", "name-suffix" and "name-title" but they are most often not. I think we want do a few things with pp:

1) put the names together (most people just use the names this way and ignore any weirdness), and then use pp to separate them and see if they separate out the same, or fix the data with the separated forms.

2) separate out fields when the "name-last" is used for the entire name, as is extremely common.

3) identify names when they are part of an organization name.

An example of what I am seeing now is this:
```
>>> import probablepeople
>>> 
>>> probablepeople.parse('Aaron Starr for Oxnard City Council')
[('Aaron', 'CorporationName'), ('Starr', 'CorporationName'), ('for', 'CorporationName'), ('Oxnard', 'CorporationName'), ('City', 'CorporationName'), ('Council', 'CorporationNameOrganization')]
>>> 
>>> probablepeople.parse('Aaron Read & Associates')
[('Aaron', 'CorporationName'), ('Read', 'CorporationName'), ('&', 'CorporationName'), ('Associates', 'CorporationNameOrganization')]
>>> 
>>> probablepeople.parse('Aaron Klein')
[('Aaron', 'GivenName'), ('Klein', 'Surname')]
>>>
```
It would have been really nice to see the following, if I could have my wish. Is there any way to get this behavior or something similar? And is there a reason "for" and "&" are 'CorporationName' and not something like a connector or conjunction? Any suggestions would be appreciated.
```
>>> import probablepeople
>>> 
>>> probablepeople.parse('Aaron Starr for Oxnard City Council')
[('Aaron', 'GivenName'), ('Starr', 'Surname'), ('for', 'Connector'), ('Oxnard', 'CorporationName'), ('City', 'CorporationName'), ('Council', 'CorporationNameOrganization')]
>>> 
>>> probablepeople.parse('Aaron Read & Associates')
[('Aaron', 'GivenName'), ('Read', 'Surname'), ('&', 'Connector'), ('Associates', 'CorporationNameOrganization')]
>>> 
```
FYI, I am also looking at cjdd3b/fec-standardizer. Perhaps that might be easier to use. We will see.

issue_title: plugins cannot be disabled when they don't exist
issue_body:
steps to reproduce:
1. create a new plugin (e.g. `hello`)
2. enable it: `kolibri plugin kolibri.plugins.hello enable`
3. delete the plugin. Now running kolibri throws an error: `ImportError: No module named hello`
4. try to disable the plugin: `kolibri plugin kolibri.plugins.hello disable` - thows an error: `kolibri.utils.cli.PluginDoesNotExist: Plugin does not exist`

At this point, `~/.kolibri/kolibri_settings.json` must be manually edited to remove the offending plugin

I'm not sure what the correct behavior is, but we probably don't want people to need to edit the settings file directly. Also, we may be able to log warnings with helpful information rather than raising exceptions.

issue_title: `charm build` should be connecting to interfaces.juju.solutions with TLS
issue_body:
The connection to interfaces.juju.solutions should be TLS-secured, since it is used to redirect `charm build` to authoritative Git repositories for each layer/interface registered. An attacker could replace legitimate layer and interface repos with their own, and inject hostile or malicious code into a charm at build time.

I recommend [Let's Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/getting-started/) if you're managing this site yourselves. It can be easily set up in minutes.

## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [ ] Provide versions of tools used
 - [ ] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following ouput:

```
$ charm version
charm 2.1.1-0ubuntu1
charm-tools 2.1.2
```

I am using: (Your operating system)

```
$ cat /etc/lsb-release 
DISTRIB_ID=Ubuntu
DISTRIB_RELEASE=16.04
DISTRIB_CODENAME=xenial
DISTRIB_DESCRIPTION="Ubuntu 16.04 LTS"
```

## Issue/Feature

### I expect/expected the following

Connections to `https://interfaces.juju.solutions`. Automatic redirect from `http://interfaces.juju.solutions` to `https://interfaces.juju.solutions` when loading the page in a browser. https:// in URLs when grepping the charmtools sources.

### What I got

No redirect. Insecure connection to `http://interfaces.juju.solutions`.

issue_title: Search
Root-node on block-sparse with non-square blocks?
issue_body:
Hi,

I am trying to solve a block sparse linear system which has non-square blocks. 
For this, I setup an sparse matrix like 

`sp_bsr = bsr_matrix((self.val, (self.idx, self.jdx)), dtype=complex, blocksize=(4,1))`

Then, I set up the root-node solver with

`ml = pyamg.rootnode_solver(A, symmetry='nonsymmetric', aggregate='standard', strength=('evolution' {'block_flag': True}), improve_candidates='sor')`

When the code wants to setup the solver I get an error like:

>  File "/home/jwittke/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyamg/relaxation/smoothing.py", line 568, in smoother
    sweep=sweep)
  File "/home/jwittke/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyamg/relaxation/relaxation.py", line 164, in sor
    gauss_seidel(A, x, b, iterations=1, sweep=sweep)
  File "/home/jwittke/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pyamg/relaxation/relaxation.py", line 332, in gauss_seidel
    raise ValueError('BSR blocks must be square')
ValueError: BSR blocks must be square


From my understanding, and what I saw in the PyAMG code, is that it will use the `improve_candidates` options for the relaxation. And also, if only one argument is stated, it will be used for all levels.
So, why is there a Gauss-Seidel relaxation still present?

In addition, my linear system comes from a pde system where four physical variables are assigned to one degree of freedom. So, every block should represent a geometrical point. Is this the best way to 
set up the sparse matrix? My problem here is, that I have a constant column block-size and a highly variable row block size.

Thank you,
Jan

issue_body:
Having worked with this library a bit more, it made more sense to remove the fetching and validation of locations from the main Configuration object. This could be replaced by a separate import e.g.

    from hdx.locations import get_valid_locations
    locations = get_valid_locations(configuration) #makes a network call to appropriate HDX endpoint
    if some_code not in locations:

This way the control over how network requests is made, or how the list of locations is cached, is the responsibility of the library user. If the locations is a plain python List this means it can also be replaced by a test object without patching or deep mocks

issue_body:
<!---

Also test if the latest release, and master branch are affected too.
-->

##### ISSUE TYPE
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->

##### OS

-->
Ubuntu Linux

```

Python : 2.7.9 (default, Jun 29 2016, 13:08:31)  [GCC 4.9.2]

```

##### ENVIRONMENT

```

```

<!--- You can also use gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### CONFIGURATION
<!--- Paste verbatim your configuration file
```

```

<!--- You can also use gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->
I'm trying to determine neighbor state by looking at the structured log from ExaBGP but it doesn't seem to be deterministic. Should this be possible?

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, please if possible, please run with the "-d" option and provide the full output,
If not possible, please provide the full fault section as reported by ExaBGP
-->
The bug might have something to do with connection collisions, so tell ExaBGP to listen (not just connect actively). Wait until you get a connection collision (you might have to reset many times) and then look at the log to see if you can successfully determine the internal state of the neighbor in ExaBGP.

Here's an example log:
```
{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1497427266, "host" : "36a4017b1b97", "pid" : "18", "ppid" : "1", "counter": 1, "type": "state", "neighbor": { "ip": "192.168.41.2", "address": { "local": "192.168.41.1", "peer": "192.168.41.2"}, "asn": { "local": "41", "peer": "2792"}, "state": "down", "reason": "connection to peer failed"} }

{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1497427274, "host" : "36a4017b1b97", "pid" : "18", "ppid" : "1", "counter": 2, "type": "state", "neighbor": { "ip": "192.168.41.2", "address": { "local": "192.168.41.1", "peer": "192.168.41.2"}, "asn": { "local": "41", "peer": "2792"}, "state": "down", "reason": "connection to peer failed"} }

{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1497427274, "host" : "36a4017b1b97", "pid" : "18", "ppid" : "1", "counter": 3, "type": "state", "neighbor": { "ip": "192.168.41.2", "address": { "local": "192.168.41.1", "peer": "192.168.41.2"}, "asn": { "local": "41", "peer": "2792"}, "state": "connected"} }

{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1497427276, "host" : "36a4017b1b97", "pid" : "18", "ppid" : "1", "counter": 4, "type": "state", "neighbor": { "ip": "192.168.41.2", "address": { "local": "192.168.41.1", "peer": "192.168.41.2"}, "asn": { "local": "41", "peer": "2792"}, "state": "up"} }

{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1497427277, "host" : "36a4017b1b97", "pid" : "18", "ppid" : "1", "counter": 5, "type": "state", "neighbor": { "ip": "192.168.41.2", "address": { "local": "192.168.41.1", "peer": "192.168.41.2"}, "asn": { "local": "41", "peer": "2792"}, "state": "connected"} }

{ "exabgp": "3.4.8", "time": 1497427278, "host" : "36a4017b1b97", "pid" : "18", "ppid" : "1", "counter": 6, "type": "state", "neighbor": { "ip": "192.168.41.2", "address": { "local": "192.168.41.1", "peer": "192.168.41.2"}, "asn": { "local": "41", "peer": "2792"}, "state": "down", "reason": "out loop, peer reset, message [notification received (6,5)] error[Cease / Connection Rejected]"} }
```
as can be seen, we reach state `up` in the fourth line but then on the fifth it says `connected` again and sixth message it says we are down. If I look from the other side (a JUNOS router) I can see that the neighbor is indeed up.

Since I'm looking at this state output to then cache it in a separate data structure (outside of ExaBGP), it will show the wrong thing. It doesn't mirror the actual state of the BGP neighbor.

Some warn to not run "show neighbors" on production ExaBGP as it stops processing - 
https://git.framasoft.org/luc/erco/blob/master/utilities/bin/exabgp-ws-client.pl#L117 - is this a valid concern?


##### EXPECTED RESULTS
<!--- What did you expect to happen when running the steps above? -->
I don't expect the neighbor state to read `connected` or `down` just because one connection attempt (in or out) was failed during the time when we had already established a different TCP connection to the neighbor.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
<!--- What actually happened? -->
See above.

##### IMPORTANCE
<!-- Please let us know if the issue is affecting you in a production environment -->

issue_title: Unable to set NN_REQ_RESEND_IVL
issue_body:
```
NN_REQ_RESEND_IVL

    This option is defined on the full REQ socket. If reply is not received in specified amount of milliseconds, the request will be automatically resent. The type of this option is int. Default value is 60000 (1 minute).
```
See http://nanomsg.org/v0.2/nn_reqrep.7.html

## `server.py`

```python
import time

import nnpy

server = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.REP)
server.bind('ipc:///tmp/foo')

time.sleep(1)
print(server.recv())
#server.send('Response from server')
print(server.recv())
```

## `client.py`

```python
import nnpy

client = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.REQ)
client.connect('ipc:///tmp/foo')
client.setsockopt(nnpy.SOL_SOCKET, nnpy.REQ_RESEND_IVL, 3000)

client.send('Request to server')
print(client.recv())
```

## Steps to reproduce

```bash
$ (time python server.py &) && python client.py
b'Request to server'
b'Request to server'

real	1m0.103s
user	0m0.031s
sys	0m0.018s
```

issue_title: Dependency hell
issue_body:
We need to work with maintainers of modules we depend on and clean-up the "dependency hell" we are in right now.  In particular, I'm now stuck with no way to fully utilize the pipeline because of ufolib dep in defcon.  But we all know what problems I'm talking about.  So let's streamline and clean up this together.  Here's my thoughts on how to do it:

  - Switch all libraries away from robofab and to defcon and ufolib.  Remove robofab dependency from defcon.  For robofab pens, either move them to fonttools and switch everyone, or make libraries that use robofab pens just make a copy for now.  Deprecate robofab and don't update it anymore and advise everyone to move away from it.

  - Switch all uses of plistlib to Python's builtin plistlib.  Defcon master currently seems to do `from ufoLib.plistlib import writePlistToString`, which fails badly if the standalone ufoLib is installed (it doesn't have plistlib).

  - Merge all py23 fixes that are outstanding.  Is there any of that left or we're good?  In particular, add the following to every Python source in every library:

  `from __future__ import print_function, division, absolute_import`

possibly also:

  `from fontTools.misc.py23 import *`

  - Add requirements.txt to all libraries.  Right now fonttools, fontmake, cu2qu, etc have, but since MutatorMath, defcon, etc do NOT, `pip install -U -r requirements.txt` is not as useful as it can be.

If we do the above, it leaves us with just one uncertainty: UFO 2 vs 3.  For that, I suggest that individual modules decided to either support both (in the same branch), or only support UFO 3.  Old versions of libraries that support UFO 2 can live in a branch forever.

So, what do people think?  Can we clean this up please?  If we agree on the action plan?

CC @jamesgk @brawer @adrientetar @anthrotype @typemytype @letterror @justvanrossum @moyogo @benkiel

issue_title: Smarter handling of train/test config differences
issue_body:
There are several cases where you want some piece of configuration to change between train time and test time.  Mostly these cases involve file dependencies in how a model is initialized - you want to have the file available at training time, but you don't need it at test time.  We partially handled this with the `files_to_archive` work, which lets you copy file dependencies into the model archive so you don't crash at test time, but there are still cases where you don't need the dependency at all after training (e.g., https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/pull/1115#discussion_r184156124, and how [pre-trained embedding files are currently handled](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/2b81da75594e7c72932687fc905d538dbe2049e8/allennlp/models/model.py#L276-L283)).  It'd be nice to handle this more cleanly.

I _think_ a reasonable way to do this would be to have a similar mechanism to [`params.add_file_to_archive`](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/2b81da75594e7c72932687fc905d538dbe2049e8/allennlp/modules/token_embedders/elmo_token_embedder.py#L82-L83), but letting you mark a parameter for deletion/modification on loading.

issue_title: Scheduling vThunder per tenant
issue_body:
Add scheduling hooks. Scheduling hooks is just an interface. It'll be called from, essentially, `Inventory.select_appliance`.

the scheduling interface should take a list of device dicts (from config), and return a list. that’s it. might also take the contexts, so it can interact with db. the return of a list is just so you can chain them. just because you pass it the device config list of devices, doesn’t mean it has to pick one of them. it just has to return a matching style dict, so the rest of the code doesn’t know the difference. you still want the same basic interface.

For the scheduling we want to decide where to put a loadbalancer, and we can't do that yet for lbaasv1 (need to do #114 for lbaasv1 to work across multiple appliances). Deciding where to put a tenant isn't the interface we want. you can always add a kwarg later with a specific object. i’d start with (context, device_list)

there should be a scheduling hook called VThunderPerTenant or something, which does the nova stuff.

issue_title: Add timeouts to every request
issue_body:
An anti-feature of the super-popular `requests` library is that it doesn't have timeouts by default. This means that if a connection hangs for whatever reason, the program itself will hang...forever. 

I filed https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/3070 about changing this in the next major version of requests, but until then, we need to add timeout values to every request. I think two of the Texas scrapers in #160 are currently frozen for just this reason.

I'll be pushing a release with this fix shortly.

issue_title: What should the structure of commands for managing Shares be?
issue_body:
This is a bit of a design question with fairly broad implications for a bunch of commands.

There's some clunkiness regarding shared endpoint management because they're _sorta_ endpoints and they're _sorta_ their own thing.
That they are valid logical endpoints, good as Task and FS op targets, is good and useful, but only if we strip back the meaning of "endpoint" to be exactly that.
Shared Endpoints don't/can't support the full set of Endpoint operations, which makes managing them as a type of endpoint very weird.

That leads to real weirdness in the API as well -- like all endpoints having a nullable "host_path" field.
At the very least, it would be nice to have endpoint subtypes added, so that we're not deducing the endpoint type from which fields are null.

Ideally, in my mind, non-shared endpoints would be "demoted" to be another specialized type of endpoint, something like "physical endpoints" or "gridftp server endpoints" (which distinguishes them from S3), but that's a bigger issue.
For now, focusing on the CLI.

I started looking at #3 again, and realized that there's actually a bit of a model problem posed here.
Regarding shared endpoint creation in particular, I think we have three options.
  1. `globus transfer endpoint create --shared --host-endpoint-id ...`
  2. `globus transfer endpoint share --host-endpoint-id ...`
  3. `globus transfer share create --host-endpoint-id ...`

If I had to distill down this issue to a TL;DR format, it would probably be a choice between those three options, phrased as
Conceptually, which of the following is a Share?

  1. A Special Type of Endpoint
  2. A Resource Belonging to an Endpoint
  3. A First-Class Entity (which happens to depend on an Endpoint)

How we think of Shared Endpoints / Shares and "what they are" will ultimately drive how we present them and manage them in the CLI and potentially other interfaces.

Right now, the API and Web App share the opinion that a Shared Endpoint is a special kind of endpoint. That works really well when you're looking at endpoints that are shared with you, but very badly when you're trying to manage your own shared endpoints.

Should this be divided into two categories?
I would be most inclined towards:

  - Your own Shared Endpoints are first-class objects which require a host-endpoint as a dependency
  - Shared Endpoints not owned by you (and even your own when found in searches) are a special case of Endpoints

That duality could be confusing if poorly presented, but I think the other split-brained view of the world -- in which managing Shared Endpoints is a confusing special case of managing an endpoint definition -- is more confusing.
Perhaps the Web UI will continue with its current layout and format, but the CLI, SDK, and other developer tools will present things in a different form? I worry that inconsistency will confuse developers by not mapping nicely to the UI, but right now we'll be confusing developers anyway, so it's not exactly a net loss.

I can take whatever kind of decision I want for now, but this needs to be resolved properly before the first "production" version.

issue_title: Creating a client with `environment="production"` does not properly translate as it would with the environment variable
issue_body:
The default config section has the "production" environment values.
We have handling code for this in `globus_sdk.config_get_default_environ`, but that only runs if `environment` isn't passed to a client.
As a result, config lookups are done against the `production` section and fail.

There are several ways of handling this, but one option which is probably pretty foolproof is to update this:
https://github.com/globus/globus-sdk-python/blob/d761ba9a104ba2d8a70d2e064d12ea95101596df/globus_sdk/base.py#L85-L86

Instead of doing that, try
```python
environment = config.get_default_environ(environment)
```
and update `get_default_environ` to take an input.

issue_title: Error processing response when request middleware short-circuits
issue_body:
The insights middleware crashes in `process_response` when a middleware short-circuits when processing the request because the `process_request` hasn't be run.

For example, the `CommonMiddleware` will short-circuit to affix a missing trailing slash with a 301 Moved Permanently response if the client accesses `/admin` when `/admin/` is the registered URL

```
MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES = [
    # ...
   'django.middleware.common.CommonMiddleware',
    # ...
   'applicationinsights.django.ApplicationInsightsMiddleware'
]
```

The reasonable solution is probably to check for the presence of the `appinsights` attribute in the `process_response` method. The method can submit the event without the `duration` field set, if the `appinsights` attribute is missing.

issue_title: Determine number of buffered rows according to memory budget
issue_body:
As a turbodbc user, I would like to specify how much memory turbodbc should use for buffering result sets, so I can deal with tables with large fields.

Context
-------

Some tables such as Exasol's `sys.exa_all_views` contain very large string columns for storing SQL queries, for example. In the aforementioned case, the length of the sole returned column was specified by the driver as 8,000,000.

With turbodbc's fixed number of rows buffered being set to 1,000 by default, the buffer for such a column amounts to roughly 8 GB.

If one could specify that the buffer target size is 200 MB, for example, turbodbc could automatically determine the number of buffered rows based on the size of individual columns.

issue_title: Implement __contains__ for TypedFeatureStructure
issue_body: TypedFeatureStructures implement __getitem__ access for keys like `t['SYNSEM.LOCAL']` or `t['SYNSEM']['LOCAL']`, so it should also provide __contains__ to check if such a key exists. That is, one could say `'SYNSEM' in t` or `'SYNSEM.LOCAL' in t` or `'LOCAL' in t['SYNSEM']`.

issue_title: Invalid JSON output
issue_body:
When installing `Jupyter` I sometimes see the following error:

```
post-link :: /etc/machine-id not found ..
bus post-link :: .. using /proc/sys/kernel/random/boot_id
```

When installing with the `--json` flag the error output causes the json to be invalid.  Example:

```
root@head:~# conda create -n test_env2 python jupyter -y --json -q
dbus post-link :: /etc/machine-id not found ..
dbus post-link :: .. using /proc/sys/kernel/random/boot_id
{
  "actions": {
    "LINK": [
      "expat-2.1.0-0 1",
      ...
    ],
    "PREFIX": "/opt/a/b/c/muunitnoc/anaconda/envs/test_env2",
    "SYMLINK_CONDA": [
      "/opt/a/b/c/muunitnoc/anaconda"
    ],
    "op_order": [
      "RM_FETCHED",
      "FETCH",
      "RM_EXTRACTED",
      "EXTRACT",
      "UNLINK",
      "LINK",
      "SYMLINK_CONDA"
    ]
  },
  "success": true
}
```

In my opinion this is a fairly critical -- I need to depend on valid JSON output

cc @kalefranz @koverholt @mingwandroid

issue_title: conda build fails running patchelf on a static binary
issue_body:
Hi,

We build a conda package including statically linked executables.
Recently, the conda build process has started failing because patchelf errors when called against a static binary:

$ patchelf --version
patchelf 0.9
$ file a.out
a.out: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, version 1 (GNU/Linux), statically linked, for GNU/Linux 2.6.32, BuildID[sha1]=5409b9978f2e5a5155090e1ac3d656f282848ad1, not stripped
$ patchelf --print-rpath a.out
cannot find section .dynamic
$ echo $?
1

See the travis build log at https://s3.amazonaws.com/archive.travis-ci.org/jobs/163653211/log.txt for an example

This seems to be a regression in conda-build, (it was working until a few days ago) but I haven't had opportunity yet to track down the change.

issue_title: Conda create fails on fresh miniconda install (lacking ~/.conda/pkgs directory)
issue_body:
## Current Behavior ``` $ conda create -n enterprise-gateway jupyter jupyter_enterprise_gateway -c conda-forge Solving environment: failed NotWritableError: The current user does not have write permissions to a required path. path: /home/edill/.conda/pkgs/urls.txt uid: 9001 gid: 9001 If you feel that permissions on this path are set incorrectly, you can manually change them by executing $ sudo chown 9001:9001 /home/edill/.conda/pkgs/urls.txt In general, it's not advisable to use 'sudo conda'. ``` ### Steps to Reproduce ``` $ wget bit.ly/get-miniconda $ bash get-miniconda -b -p ~/miniconda $ ~/miniconda/bin/conda activate # this one fails with the following message CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'. If your shell is Bash or a Bourne variant, enable conda for the current user with $ echo ". /home/edill/miniconda/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" >> ~/.bashrc or, for all users, enable conda with $ sudo ln -s /home/edill/miniconda/etc/profile.d/conda.sh /etc/profile.d/conda.sh The options above will permanently enable the 'conda' command, but they do NOT put conda's base (root) environment on PATH. To do so, run $ conda activate in your terminal, or to put the base environment on PATH permanently, run $ echo "conda activate" >> ~/.bashrc Previous to conda 4.4, the recommended way to activate conda was to modify PATH in your ~/.bashrc file. You should manually remove the line that looks like export PATH="/home/edill/miniconda/bin:$PATH" ^^^ The above line should NO LONGER be in your ~/.bashrc file! ^^^ ``` Then we add the conda.sh to the bashrc and source it: ``` $ echo ". /home/edill/miniconda/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" >> ~/.bashrc $ source ~/.bashrc $ conda activate (base) $ conda update conda Solving environment: failed NotWritableError: The current user does not have write permissions to a required path. path: /home/edill/.conda/pkgs/urls.txt uid: 9001 gid: 9001 If you feel that permissions on this path are set incorrectly, you can manually change them by executing $ sudo chown 9001:9001 /home/edill/.conda/pkgs/urls.txt In general, it's not advisable to use 'sudo conda'. ``` That's weird. Check permissions: ``` $ ll total 57100 -rw-rw-r--. 1 edill edill 58468498 Jun 7 05:26 get-miniconda drwxrwxr-x. 13 edill edill 197 Aug 27 13:32 miniconda $ ls -la total 57116 drwx------. 4 edill edill 135 Aug 27 13:33 . drwxr-xr-x. 5 root root 52 Aug 27 13:27 .. -rw-------. 1 edill edill 265 Aug 27 13:32 .bash_history -rw-r--r--. 1 edill edill 18 Aug 3 2017 .bash_logout -rw-r--r--. 1 edill edill 193 Aug 3 2017 .bash_profile -rw-r--r--. 1 edill edill 278 Aug 27 13:32 .bashrc drwxrwxr-x. 2 edill edill 6 Aug 27 13:33 .conda -rw-rw-r--. 1 edill edill 58468498 Jun 7 05:26 get-miniconda drwxrwxr-x. 13 edill edill 197 Aug 27 13:32 miniconda $ ls -la .conda total 0 drwxrwxr-x. 2 edill edill 6 Aug 27 13:33 . drwx------. 4 edill edill 135 Aug 27 13:33 .. ``` No .pkgs directory in the .conda directory. Let's create that and try again. ``` $ mkdir .conda/pkgs $ conda update conda Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/edill/miniconda added / updated specs: - conda The following packages will be UPDATED: certifi: 2018.4.16-py36_0 --> 2018.8.13-py36_0 conda: 4.5.4-py36_0 --> 4.5.10-py36_0 openssl: 1.0.2o-h20670df_0 --> 1.0.2p-h14c3975_0 Proceed ([y]/n)? y Preparing transaction: done Verifying transaction: failed NotWritableError: The current user does not have write permissions to a required path. path: /home/edill/.conda/environments.txt uid: 9001 gid: 9001 If you feel that permissions on this path are set incorrectly, you can manually change them by executing $ sudo chown 9001:9001 /home/edill/.conda/environments.txt In general, it's not advisable to use 'sudo conda'. ``` ## Expected Behavior `conda update conda` makes all required directories and files and works out of the box. ## Environment Information

issue_title: restore is stuck and never exit even when status is "DONE"
issue_body:
New bug related to #962 
restore is stuck and never exit even when status is "DONE"
```
Index "index_v0.2_2017-02-12_536a9247f9fa4fc7a7942ad46ea14e0d" is still in stage "DONE
```
```
actions:
  1:
    action: restore
    description: restore via curator
    options:
      repository: force-merge
      name: force-merge
      extra_settings:
        settings:
          number_of_shards: 1
          number_of_replicas: 0
      wait_for_completion: True
      include_aliases: True
      max_wait: 3600
      wait_interval: 10
      ignore_unavailable: False
      include_global_state: False
      disable_action: False
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: prefix
      value: force-
```

more info:
The restore is done via a single elasticsearch  node with ```node.master: true```. 
index size: 24G
curator: 5.0.3

issue_title: CentOS 7 RPM installation fails: "package is not signed"
issue_body:
Hi all,

I'm not sure if this belongs here. But I don't know where else to go. I try to install the curator RPM under CentOS 7 and followed the instructions under https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/installation.html#yum-repository

```
[root@salttestvm70 ~]# cat /etc/yum.repos.d/curator-3.repo
[curator-3]
gpgcheck=1

issue_title: Improving speed performance to keep up with Kafka ingestion
issue_body:
Currently, I have a function that looks at the data individually as it comes in and performs updates, deletes, and inserts.
But it takes 1/10 of a second for each action and that's too slow for ingestion. What ways can I speed this up? I considered the BULK API but I'm not sure how a generator would help me when there's no real 'fixed' list.

```
def esInsertLogic(msg):
	a = datetime.now()
	if 'tenantid' in msg['data']: #If it has a tenant is present in the message received
		index = 'test_p2'#Create the index value to use in ES
		logger.debug("Found index: "+ str(index))
		esid = msg['data']['id'] #Save the id in the msg received
		b = datetime.now()
		if es.indices.exists(index=index): #If Index Exists
			c = datetime.now()
			#Case 1: UPDATE
			if msg['type'] == "update": # If Update Required
				try: #Try updating
					q = datetime.now()
					old = es.get(index=index, doc_type=msg['table'], id=esid) # Find the old data
					w = datetime.now()
					print "Finding Old Data:", w-q
					if 'ts' in old.keys(): #if the old data has a timestamp
						if old['ts'] < msg['ts']: # if ts is larger than old ts update, else ignore
							es.update(index=index,doc_type=msg['table'],id=esid, body={'doc':msg['data']})
							logger.debug("SUCCESS: Updated index "+ str(index)+ ", id "+ str(esid))
				except Exception as e: #if unable to update
					failed = True
					logger.error(e)
					try:
						es.index(index=index, doc_type=msg['table'], id=esid, body=msg['data']) #Try inserting it into index
						failed=False
					except Exception as e:
						logger.error("UPDATE/INSERT ERROR"+str(e))
						failed=True
					if failed == True: #If all fails, Log it for further review
						logger.error("UPDATE OVERALL ERROR: \n"+ str(e)+ "\n"+str(msg))
			d = datetime.now()
			#Case 2: INSERT
			if msg['type'] == "insert":
				try:
					es.index(index=index, doc_type=msg['table'], id=esid, body=msg['data'])
					logger.debug("Inserted index "+ str(index)+ ", id "+ str(esid))
				except Exception as e:
					logger.error("INSERT ERROR\n"+str(e)+str(msg))
			e = datetime.now()
			#Case 3: DELETE
			if msg['type'] == "delete":
				try:
					es.delete(index=index, doc_type=msg['table'], id=esid)
					logger.debug("Deleted index "+ str(index)+ ", id "+ str(esid))
				except Exception as e:
					logger.error("DELETE ERROR\n")
					logger.error("Data replacement: "+str(e)+ str(msg))
			print "Update:", d-c
			print "Insert:", e-d
			print "Delete:", datetime.now()-e
		else: #If Index doesn't exists in ES
			g= datetime.now()
			print "Creating Index", index
			createIndex = es.indices.create(index=index) #Create index in ES
			logger.debug("Creating Index "+ str(index)+ ": "+ str(createIndex))
			es.index(index=index, doc_type=msg['table'], id=esid, body=msg['data']) #Insert data into index
			logger.debug("Inserted into "+ str(index))
			print "Insert new index:", datetime.now()-g
	else:
		logger.warning("No specified index in JSON") #If not warning sent out
		logger.debug(msg['data'])
		logger.debug(" ============= \n")
```

issue_title: [DNS] 'show' on empty record set doesn't show empty record list
issue_body:
If I do 'az network dns record-set show', I get shown the record set details. That includes the list of records in the record set.

However, if the record set is empty (no records), then the empty record set list is NOT shown. That's bad. I'm left looking for it. It's quite a jump to realize the record set must have no records because the list isn't shown.

By contrast, if there is no metadata associated with the record set, we DO show an empty metadata list, to make that explicit.

Empty record sets (of all types) should show their empty record list explicitly. Note that the list property name varies with record type. This applies to the 'show' command and also any other command that displays record set data (e.g. list, or when creating an empty record set, etc)

issue_title: Improvements to --ids parameter
issue_body:
**1. Ingest Output Formats Easily**

Currently `--ids` requires a space-separate list of IDs.  To feed this to from another command, you could try:

```
az foo action --ids $(az foo list --query [].id --output tsv)
```
This doesn't actually work.  It would be much more convenient if --ids could sniff IDs from a variety of output formats, to enable the following:

```
az foo action --ids $(az foo list)
```

**2. Allow Looping on Name**

Currently you can supply a list of IDs... OR a single name. When I would like to delete multiple things in the same resource group, it would be convenient to allow the name component to accept a list of names.

```
az foo show -g {rg} -n name1 name2 name3
```
Should work just like:
```
az foo show --ids id1 id2 id3
```

issue_title: `az storage blob upload` doesn't perform sparse upload
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

Given a sparse image.vhd:

```
$ ls -lah image.vhd 
-rw-r--r-- 1 ubuntu ubuntu 31G Jul  9 15:35 image.vhd
$ du -h image.vhd 
1015M	image.vhd
```

Running

```
az storage blob upload <snip> --type page --file image.vhd --name image.vhd
```

takes substantially longer than uploading a file that contains only the non-sparse parts of the image. This strongly suggests to me that (a) the upload isn't taking advantage of the file's sparseness, and (b) the file as stored in Azure won't be sparse (making operation on it more expensive in the future).

**To Reproduce**
Create a sparse file and upload it.

**Expected behavior**
The upload would take the amount of time that uploading the non-sparse part of the file takes, and would be stored as sparse in Azure.

**Environment summary**

pip installed on Ubuntu 18.10

azure.storage.QueueMessage considered harmful
issue_body:
This week I wrote code addressing Azure Storage Blobs, Tables and Queues. Great stuff. Since I ran into errors while removing message that were dequeued more often than my limit, I looked into the details of azure.storage.QueueMessage. The implementation was surprising me...

azure.storage.QueueMessage is just a bunch of strings. I was really surprised that this class (obviously nothing more than a Data Transfer Object) ships with an SDK that is marked as 'production/stable/ on PyPI. 


class QueueMessage(WindowsAzureData):

    ''' Queue message class. '''

    def __init__(self):
        self.message_id = u''
        self.insertion_time = u''
        self.expiration_time = u''
        self.pop_receipt = u''
        self.time_next_visible = u''
        self.dequeue_count = u''
        self.message_text = u''

I would expect members having names containing 'time' to have type DateTime and members having names indicating an integer (dequeue_count) to be an Integer. Instances of this class are just prone to error. That's what happened to me.

Focused Python developers may expect a standard-level pythonic API coming from Microsoft that of course will hide transport implementations details as well that are discussed in this issue [https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/150]. 

Can anybody comment this or explain why it is like that or even has to be like that? Any suggestions how to contribute on that important topic without breaking APIs are welcome.

issue_title: Managed Identity/IMDS auth: Configurable retry time-out.
issue_body:
Currently, the retry logic in the Azure SDKs for Managed Identity token generation can make the current thread wait for as long as 15 minutes. For response time sensitive services this might not be acceptable. It could also result in service unavailability if all threads are blocked for a token to be generated when a sudden burst arrives after a cold start.
The developer may want to fast fail after certain time interval says for example 10 seconds if the call is still failing.


Can we make the retry max configurable?

issue_title: Faster (than) git clone
issue_body:
Hi,

I was wondering how `pre-commit install` execution time could be improved.
Especially, I was considering the [`git clone` command employed](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/eb2ac28f20d8732ccdb60bb41efe0acf71165fdf/pre_commit/store.py#L142-L152) to retrieve pre-commit hooks repos.
Hence a few questions:
- is there any specific reason to fetch the whole repo ? A necessity to have its full commit history fetched ?
- could it be considered to only retrieve a single commit ?
  Using for exemple one of the 2 methods mentioned here: http://stackoverflow.com/a/26957305/636849
- could we add a heuristic that retrieve archives from Github repos ?
  cf. https://developer.github.com/v3/repos/contents/#get-archive-link

```
https://github.com/User/repo/tarball/$sha
https://github.com/User/repo/zipball/$sha
```
- maybe advise hooks repo owner to `git bundle create` their repos to make cloning faster ?

I'm not git specialist, but I think some improvment could be done without much cost (simply adding `--depth 1` to the `git clone` may be an easy win).

issue_title: additonal_dependencies isn't "rollback safe"
issue_body: Using old pre-commit + a hook repo with `additional_dependencies` it'll happily create the repo without installing the additional dependencies.  Upon upgrading to a newer pre-commit, it doesn't know that the additional dependencies aren't installed yet and will happily attempt to run in there (usually causing an executable to not be found).  We need some way to signify when these have been installed in order for this to be rollable.  A workaround is to `pre-commit clean` when upgrading, but that kinda is not the best (and especially confusing).

`m_shapeType` should be `MINKOWSKI_SUM_SHAPE_PROXYTYPE` not `MINKOWSKI_DIFFERENCE_SHAPE_PROXYTYPE`

issue_title: [client] 301 in POST gets redirected to GET
issue_body:
## Long story short

Using `aiohttp-client` to make a `POST` request, if the server responds with a `301` the client makes a new request to the provided URI but the new request is an empty `GET`.

## Expected behaviour

According to [`RFC2616-sec10.3.2`](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html#sec10.3.2) the method should not change. Emphasis on:

> When automatically redirecting a POST request after
      receiving a 301 status code, some existing HTTP/1.0 user agents
      will **erroneously** change it into a GET request.

## Actual behaviour

According to [`RFC1945-sec9.3`](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1945#section-9.3), [`RFC2068-sec10.3`](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2068#section-10.3) and [`RFC2616-sec10.3`](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html#sec10.3):

> The action required MAY be carried out by the user agent without interaction with the user if and only if the method used in the second request is GET or HEAD

Now, considering the in-code nature of `aiohttp-client` (i.e. it's not exactly an end-user user-agent) and the fact that `aiohttp-client` provides an option to not follow redirects, one could argue that the above should not strictly apply.

So, the way I see it there are two choices:

- Maintain the HTTP method when redirecting (this is what happens for example when `aiohttp-client` gets a `308` during a `POST` request)

- Do not redirect on any method other that `GET` and `HEAD` (event if follow redirects is `True`)

## Steps to reproduce

Using the following server - client pair

```py
import asyncio

from aiohttp import web

async def rt(request):
    return web.HTTPOk()

@web.middleware
async def print_request(request, handler):

    print(request)
    return (await handler(request))

async def get_app():
    middlewares = [print_request, web.web_middlewares.normalize_path_middleware()]
    app = web.Application(middlewares=middlewares)
    app.router.add_route('POST', '/rt/', rt)
    return app

web.run_app(get_app())
```

```py
import aiohttp
import asyncio

async def pt():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.post('http://localhost:8080/rt', data=b'data') as resp:
            print(resp.status)

asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(pt())
```

The client will print:

```
405
```

Indicating a Method Not Allowed (since it used GET on the resource)

and the server will print:

```
<Request POST /rt >                                 
<Request GET /rt/ >
```

Indicating that after the first `POST` on the resource the client tried to `GET` the resource returned by the server.

## Your environment

Ubuntu 17.10
aiohttp 3.3.1

issue_title: RuntimeError: set_wakeup_fd only works in main thread
issue_body:
## Long story short

I'm trying to run an aiohttp `Application` in a deamon thread. I use `asyncio.get_child_watcher()` in the main thread so subprocesses will be allowed to run. but I get `RuntimeError: set_wakeup_fd only works in main thread`

## Expected behaviour

Running aiohttp Application in a worker thread 

## Actual behaviour

RuntimeError is raised

## Steps to reproduce

A little more of context:
I'm writing tests for an aiohttp application. This app calls an external rest-full API to get some data. Now I'm trying to create another aiohttp application in a daemon thread to mock that third-party application behaviour. 
This is my `MockServer` class:

`class MockingServer(object):
    def __init__(self, host, port):
        self.host = host
        self.port = port
        self.app = Application()
        r = Resource('/validate_get/{content_uid}/{user_uuid}', Route('GET', '/validate_get', self.mock_handler))
        resource = self.app.router.add_resource(r.path)
        resource.add_route(r.route.method, r.route.handler)

    async def mock_handler(self, request):
        return json_response({'ok': True})

    def start(self):
        get_child_watcher()
        thread = Thread(target=self._start, daemon=True)
        thread.start()

    def _start(self, *args):
        loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
        asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
        run_app(self.app, port=self.port, loop=loop)` 


## Your environment
Python 3.5.2
OSX
aiohttp

issue_title: TimeoutError: Proper way to read chunk wise?
issue_body:
## Long story short

- When fetching files chunk-wise, we get `TimeoutError` - dependent on hardware (processing power, chunk size) sooner or later. E.g. On a MBP 2017, Python 3.65 + aiohttp 3.3 while reading a 10GB file chunk wise at 512KB per `read`. On an embedded Linux w/ limited processing power + 512 bytes chunk size much earlier (e.g. 512Bytes / 80MB file).
- See https://github.com/rauc/rauc-hawkbit/pull/8.
- Same happens with using `read_chunk` - what's the proper way to read chunk wise?

## Expected behaviour

- No `TimeoutError` exception

## Actual behaviour

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "aiohttp-rauc-hawkbit.py", line 30, in <module>
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
  File "/usr/local/opt/asdf/installs/python/3.6.5/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py", line 468, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "aiohttp-rauc-hawkbit.py", line 22, in main
    await fetch(session, 'https://speed.hetzner.de/10GB.bin')
  File "/usr/local/opt/asdf/installs/python/3.6.5/lib/python3.6/asyncio/coroutines.py", line 129, in throw
    return self.gen.throw(type, value, traceback)
  File "aiohttp-rauc-hawkbit.py", line 8, in fetch
    chunk = await response.content.read(512000)
  File "/usr/local/opt/asdf/installs/python/3.6.5/lib/python3.6/asyncio/coroutines.py", line 129, in throw
    return self.gen.throw(type, value, traceback)
  File "/Users/livioso/.local/share/virtualenvs/third-party-api-experiments-pge9BDZy/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/streams.py", line 319, in read
    await self._wait('read')
  File "/usr/local/opt/asdf/installs/python/3.6.5/lib/python3.6/asyncio/coroutines.py", line 129, in throw
    return self.gen.throw(type, value, traceback)
  File "/Users/livioso/.local/share/virtualenvs/third-party-api-experiments-pge9BDZy/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/streams.py", line 250, in _wait
    await waiter
  File "/Users/livioso/.local/share/virtualenvs/third-party-api-experiments-pge9BDZy/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/helpers.py", line 671, in __exit__
    raise asyncio.TimeoutError from None
concurrent.futures._base.TimeoutError
```

## Steps to reproduce

```
import aiohttp
import asyncio


async def fetch(session, url):
    async with session.get(url) as response:
        while True:
            chunk = await response.content.read(512000)

            # a) same issue, eventually breaks
            # chunk, _ = await response.content.readchunk()

            # b) no effect
            # asyncio.sleep(0.001)

            if not chunk:
                break


async def main():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        await fetch(session, 'https://speed.hetzner.de/10GB.bin')
        # await fetch(session, 'http://localhost:2280/huge.zip')
        # await fetch(session, 'http://localhost:2280/segin-rootfs-update-02.raucb')
        # await fetch(session, 'https://speed.hetzner.de/100MB.bin')
        print('Success')

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.set_debug(True)
loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

## Your environment

- Python 3.6.5
- aiohttp 3.3 (latest)

issue_title: Multiply-wrapped elements over-indented
issue_body:
Currently, the notebook wraps double-wrapped elements as:

    a = np.array([
            1,
            2,
        ])

That is, it doubles up the indentation.

Compare this to the behavior of PyCharm:

    a = np.array([
        1,
        2,
    ])

Sublime Text takes a third approach:

    a = np.array([
                 1,
                 2,
                 ])

As far as I can tell, PEP8 doesn't have a recommendation here, so I recognize this is ambiguous. Nevertheless, I find the PyCharm behavior ideal. To me, Sublime's behavior is the worst of the three, *However*, once you fix the second line (with the `1` in my examples), further newlines will start  at the same indentation level. In the notebook, even after adjusting the `1` line, the `2` line starts one level deeper. Even if there's no consensus as to which default behavior is best, ideally the current indentation should be picked up on.

issue_title: numpy.take and duplicate indices
issue_body:
Hi,

First of all, thanks for making autograd! It's been extremely useful.  

I noticed though that np.take produces the wrong result if the index array contains duplicate values.  For example, 
`quick_grad_check(lambda x: np.sum(x[[0,0]]), np.array([1.]))`
fails with:
`AssertionError: Check failed! nd=2.0, ad=1.0`

You can fix it by changing a line in `primitive_sum_arrays` (in `numpy_extra.py`) from
`new_array[array.idx] += array.val`
to:
`numpy.add.at(new_array, array.idx, array.val)`.  I'm not sure if this error occurs elsewhere, but it seems to at least fix the problem in all of the cases that I've tried.

issue_title: stop using bare except:
issue_body: A more recent version of flake8 has started failing on bare except, which is very reasonable. https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/tree/bareexcept is a first attempt to get rid of them. That fails a git plugin test, because previously we were using bare except to suppress a `sys.exit` call. (Horrifying!) We need to clean up the shell-out helper a little bit, so that callers that want to handle errors have the option of doing that.

issue_title: Proposals for Blockstore scaling
issue_body:
@muneeb-ali @jcnelson @guylepage3 and I all had a discussion about the updates we can make to scale Blockstore, and I would like to continue our discussion here.

We discussed a bunch of ideas, and here are the ones I suggest we focus on:
#### 1. Use mutable storage for profile updates

At the moment, each profile update results in a transaction on the bitcoin network, updating the immutable data item associated with a name.

In the very near future, we are going to move to a system where the data item associated with a name is simply instructions for how to resolve names to profile data. This is just like the zone files used by the traditional DNS system, which provide instructions for how to resolve names to IP addresses.

This allows us to cut our update transactions down to 1 per user upon registration, plus 1 for each time a user updates its data storage provider.
#### 2. Pack multiple name registrations into OP_RETURN transactions

We could start packing multiple name registration operations into each transaction. We could have the operation data start with the prefix and end with a TLD that applies to all names in the operation. Then, in the center we pack all the names and separate them by commas.

In addition, we could bump up our OP_RETURN data usage from 40 bytes to 80 bytes, giving us even more room.

For perspective, we'd be able to register the following number of names:

```
2 x 37-character names
4 x 18-character names
6 x 12-character names
8 x 9-character names
10 x 7-character names
12 x 6-character names
```

_Note: the character counts given above do not include the TLDs_
#### 3. Implement operation packing for updates, revokes, and renews

We've discussed how operation packing for updates is very simple. Each update tree can be processed independently and if a tree gets missed, the system can still make progress.

The same is actually true for revokes and renews.

Transfers are more complicated, however. If one transfer is missed, nodes cannot know that they have the right name ownership information.
#### 4. Implement operation chaining to allow for transfer packing

We can support transfer packing by using name-specific operation chaining, where each name operation references the previous operation. Thus, if a node sees a transfer or update, it will be able to determine whether it was valid or not because it can simply see if it has the full chain since registration.

Registration packing, though, is a completely different beast. Essentially, missing individual name registrations is an unacceptable scenario and results in very bad things. Thus, perfect operation tree broadcasting is a pre-requisite. We've discussed ways to accomplish this, but it seems that things got complicated very quickly and I don't think we've fully thought through all possible failure scenarios. There's a much longer conversation to be had here, but I think we should push off registration packing for now.
#### Where this will get us

At the current block size of 1MB, miners can pack up to about 2000 transactions per block. At that rate, the bitcoin blockchain can support 100M transactions per year.

```
2000*144*365 = 105,120,000
```

Conceivably, in the best best case scenario of blockchain ID registration growth, we could make up 10% of Bitcoin transaction volume, in which case, we'd be able to do 10M transactions per year at current block sizes.

If all non-registration operations were packed and all preorders were bulk preorders, the vast majority of this (99.9%) would be registration transactions. That means that 5M name registration transactions could be issued per year.

Now... it is very realistic that blocks will hit 8MB in 5 years time. In that case, we'd be able to do 80M name registrations per year (if we packed two names in per TX), which should be more than enough.

Remember that these calculations are just for ensuring that we can handle a scenario where this system gets as big as Facebook. **This is not expected, and so we do not have any expectations or plans to clog up bitcoin transaction volume.**

issue_body:
Ported from https://github.com/NSLS-II/metadatastore/issues/256:

Enable users to add comments at any time after a run that are loaded with the header.

```python3
# Add a comment associated with zero, one, or more uids.

>>> uids = RE(...)
>>> db.mds.comment(uids, string, **any_kwargs_you_want)

# Or, to easily comment on the runs from the last RE call, an IPython magic:

I ran this by a couple BL scientists and it was well-received. Apparently this is a SPEC feature that we are still missing.

issue_title: New configuration system spec draft
issue_body:
Goal: Support multiple databrokers in the same process so it is easy to switch between beamlines or combine data between beamlines or juggle different databrokers for raw and analyzed data.

Plan, previously outlined with @tacaswell and @stuartcampbell on a whiteboard:

Deprecate `databroker.DataBroker` and `databroker.db` with noisy warnings. (We probably have to maintain them for a long time or we will break ~1000 user notebooks.)

The new encouraged usage will be:

```python
from databroker import Broker
db = Broker.named('chx')
```

relying on a config file something like:

```yaml
# /etc/databroker/chx.yml
headersource:  # mapped to one dict
  module: databroker.headersource.mongo
  class: MDSRO
  config:
    database: metdatastore-production-v1
    host: xf23id-broker
    port: 27017
    timezone: US/Eastern
    
event_sources:  # mapped to a list of dicts (in this case, a list with one element)
  - module: databroker.headersource.mongo   # in the future, this will a proper EventSource
    class: MDSRO
    config:
      database: metdatastore-production-v1
      host: xf23id-broker
      port: 27017
      timezone: US/Eastern
    filestore:
      module: databroker.filestore.mongo
      class: FileStoreRO
      config:
        database: filestore-production-v1
        host: xf23id-broker
        port: 27017
```

New broker code would look something like:

```python
SEARCH_PATH = ('/etc/databroker', os.path.expanduser('~/.config/databroker/'))

def lookup_config(name):
    # Look for a file called {name}.yml in the SEARCH_PATH.
    # Open and parse it into a dict
    return config

from importlib import import_module

class Broker:
    ...
    @classmethod
    def from_config(cls, config):
        modname = config['headersource']['module']  # a string
        clsname = config['headersource']['class']  # a string
        config = config['headersource']['config']  # a dict
        mod = import_module(modname)
        cls = getattr(mod, clsname)
        headersource = cls(config)
        # similar for event_source(s) and their filestores
        return cls(headersource, *eventsources)

    @classmethod
    def named(cls, name):
        return cls.from_config(lookup_config(name))
```

issue_title: Error in Google order style (I100)
issue_body:
A change in the 0.9 release broke the Google order style, at least for `I100`.  It looks like it is not ignoring the case of letters anymore.

According to the [Google Python Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html?showone=Imports_formatting#Imports_formatting)
> Within each grouping, imports should be sorted lexicographically, ignoring case, according to each module's full package path.

Here is an example that worked in 0.8, but is flagging `I100` in 0.9:
```python
import os
import StringIO
import sys
```

This (incorrect) example passes in 0.9:
```python
import StringIO
import os
import sys
```

issue_title: Operations fail because of spurious generation mismatch error.
All class names must be standardized
TrustDomain needs to be aware of status of the device service group: device_trust_group
issue_body: When clustering, the first step is to create a trust relationship between all devices in the cluster. This is done through a pre-built device service group called 'device_trust_group' and it is only used for peering. The TrustDomain module needs to be aware of this group and how to manage it. It needs to be able to sync to the group and check the status. This is accomplished in the multi_device/device_group module. So it either needs to instantiate that to manage the group or have it passed in.

issue_title: Calling 3rd party DLL that makes calls out to the internet from .Net core fails proxy authentication despite configuring or coding the required settings
issue_body:
I have created a .Net Core 2.0 Web.API that will relay a request to a 3rd party provider via their .Net 4.0 DLL which in turn handles the communication with their Web API and returns the results.

In a simple .Net 4 application this all works perfectly and when I run my Web.API and make calls to it using a direct internet connection again it all works perfectly.

However, if it is run connecting to our work network which uses a proxy I get 407 Proxy Authentication Required. Fair enough all I need do is specify that I want to use the default credentials and it should all work. So A bit of digging around had given me several possible solutions. Unfortunately under .Net Core 2.0 they don't work.

I have tried deploying to my local machine and specifying in the web.Config:

`<system.net>
  <defaultProxy useDefaultCredentials="true" />
</system.net>`

I have also tried adding this configuration to the machine.config (which seemed a bit overkill) again no joy.

I have also tried adding to my code in my Web API:

`System.Net.WebRequest.DefaultWebProxy.Credentials = System.Net.CredentialCache.DefaultNetworkCredentials;
`

both before and after instantiating a reference to the 3rd party DLL and still when the vendors DLL makes its call to their Web API, via HTTP or HTTPS I get back 407 Proxy Authentication Required.

In an attempt to prove that I was not going mad I stripped the core of the work out to a standard .Net application to create basic a test harness and applying either of the solutions to that program it all works as expected. I have also created a separate .Net 4 DLL to act as a wrapper to the 3rd party DLL and in its constructor put in the above code to set the credentials in an attempt to isolate the effect to that environment. Again, this works perfectly under a simple test application but fails under the .Net Core Web.API.

Putting fiddler into the equation to watch the communication with the working application you can see the initial request get denied and proxy authentication get requested, the authentication then gets passed back and the actual request then gets through and the result passed back.

But in the .Net core Web.API I have developed the request simply goes out, hits the proxy authentication request, but then no credentials get pushed back to get things going and the conversation terminates.

This is the first time that I have ever worked with .Net Core so I may be missing something that I need to do, but from my perspective it looks like when I set-up the environment in my application for proxy authentication that same environment/settings are not being passed through to the DLL's environment and so I can't handle the request properly.

So I need some way of setting up or injecting the environment for the 3rd party DLL to ensure that it knows what to do with the authentication request via System.Net.WebRequest.DefaultWebProxy or similar.

So far I have tried running the code in a completely different thread and tried creating a completely separate .Net standard DLL to act as a wrapper, inside that DLL I then use code to force the System.Net.WebRequest.DefaultWebProxy.Credentials to use the default network credentials. Plugging either of these into my test harness they work but as soon as they are put into the .Net Core Web API they fail in exactly the same way.

issue_title: Asks about HTTPS redirect despite installer not supporting it
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm writing an installer plugin that will not support enhancements, and thus returns none:

    def supported_enhancements(self):
        return []

Nevertheless, on letsencrypt install (with this plugin), I get the choice “Please choose whether HTTPS access is required or optional.”, and then I can choose between Easy or Secure. Shouldn't it check with the installer if it can do the “redirect” enhancement before it asks? It just calls enhance() with no more ifs and buts, which fails.

issue_title: Multiple renewals with standalone don't work
issue_body:
If you create two separate lineages with the standalone configurator, and then try to renew both with the new `letsencrypt renew`, the second one will fail because the port is already in use.

The standalone plugin does try to stop the listening server when the challenge has been satisfied, but it seems that this fails to release the port. We may need to find a way to explicitly set `SO_REUSEADDR` on the listening socket (before it's bound!) in order to allow a listening socket to be immediately rebound.

issue_title: Narrative documentation for plugins
issue_body: While changes proposed in #1079 help, it is very hard to tell from the names `manual`, `standalone`, and `webroot` what our plugins actually do. We need to add narrative documentation for each plugin to help people understand the purpose of each plugin and when they should be used.

Error constructing the URL for django-storages when using django-configurations and other
issue_body:
Hi,

When using django-configurations it happens that URL are malformed
```
<script src="https://s3.amazonaws.com/<Value default: None>/ckeditor/ckeditor/ckeditor.js" type="text/javascript"></script>
```
This happens because when using [django-configurations](https://github.com/jezdez/django-configurations) usually the bucket name is set as
```
AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = values.SecretValue()
```
Then inside [widgets.py](https://github.com/django-ckeditor/django-ckeditor/blob/e8b9df963f4b66eff86df8cdb89e6623f5b33f64/ckeditor/widgets.py) the injected media is

```
try:
            js += (
                settings.STATIC_URL + 'ckeditor/ckeditor/ckeditor.js',
                settings.STATIC_URL + 'ckeditor/ckeditor-init.js',
            )

```
I think a better approach is to use 
```
from django.contrib.staticfiles.templatetags.staticfiles import static
url = static('ckeditor/ckeditor/ckeditor.js')
```

best,

issue_title: Flawed sync --keepDays semantics
issue_body:
Currently `b2 sync --keepDays 30` deletes file versions older than 30 days, by looking at the modification time of the file. This is fatally flawed. If I have a file that was last modified a year ago, and I accidentally delete it, and sync, the sync will delete the file (because version.modtime = 1 year, > 30 days), leaving only a "hidden" marker. Even though I asked to keep 30 days of history, I can't undo a mistake I did a minute ago and my data is gone.

I think it should use the upload timestamp instead.

issue_title: Deprecate allow_null parameter of fields.Nested?
Rename class Meta to class Options
issue_body:
Like [Schematics](https://schematics.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage/models.html).

```python
class Meta
```
is too similar to \_\_metaclass__ or class MyClass(metaclass=...).

class Options is more semantically accurate for what this object is, and there's precedent for using that name, so I support renaming it.  For backward compatibility, marshmallow could check for the presence of either.

issue_title: NREL SPA license problems
issue_body:
It appears to me that we may be in violation of NREL's SPA [license](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/spa_c_files/SPA_NOTICE.md)

"The Software is being provided for internal, noncommercial purposes only and shall not be re-distributed."

A couple of potential problems:
* We are redistributing their code rather than just making binaries with it.
* We (or at least I) don't want to limit pvlib-python to non-commerical purposes. Everything else in pvlib-python is, to my knowledge, able to be used in commercial applications.

The license goes on to say that you must contact them about using it in commercial applications.

Am I reading this wrong? @Calama-Consulting did you have a different interpretation of this when you added the SPA code to pvlib?

issue_title: [72] Quotes included in the node's name
issue_body:
**Reported by wod...@gmail.com, 2012-05-07T15:30:05Z**

##### What steps will reproduce the problem?
1) Create a dot file with lines:
```
  "testnode"
  testnode 
```
2) call get_nodes()

##### What is the expected output? What do you see instead?
I would expect one node called 'testnode'. Not two - 'testnode' and '"testnode"' (quoted). Quotes should not be included in the names.

##### What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
pydot-1.0.28

Possibly associated with Issue 66.

Dot spec. states:
An ID is just a string; the lack of quote characters in the first two forms is just for simplicity. There is no semantic difference between abc_2 and "abc_2", or between 2.34 and "2.34".

http://www.graphviz.org/content/dot-language
From: https://code.google.com/p/pydot/issues/detail?id=72

issue_title: How to create a factory with an argument and default arguments?
issue_body:
I want to create a factory which calls the constructor `NoDownloader(state, parse_content, subclasses)` where `state` must be explicitly specified on every factory call, but `parse_context` and `subclasses` have default values (when calling the factory).

That is I want my factory `f` work for such calls:

* `f(state)`
* `f(state, parse_context=..., subclasses=...)`

It is also desirable that `state` is the _first_ argument of `NoDownloader`, to be able to specify it as a positional argument even when there are other arguments (`parse_context`, `subclasses`). However, this is not very important because `NoDownloader` constructor should be called just in one point of the program (the factory `f`).

issue_title: Provide access to RDS
issue_body:
RDS instances, when they are not public, can only be accessed from certain machines, for example, from the Odd Host. The solution is an SSH tunnel:

    $ ssh -N -L 45432:<rds_instance_url>:<rds_port> <username>@odd.example.com

This makes  the RDS port be available as 45432 on the local machine.
But in order to get access to the Odd Host itself, one has to request access to some other Taupage-based machine, like:

    $ piu <ip_address> "need to access RDS"

This works, but having to refer to an irrelevant Taupage-based service seems redundant.

It would be nice to have the following syntax:

    $ piu --db <port> "need to access RDS"

that will just provision user's public key to the Odd Host only and print a command line for `ssh -N -L ...`

issue_title: confusion between <stack-name> & <stack-name> - <version>
issue_body:
different senza commands have different params regarding stack&version - some may just require version (create), some stack (delete), and some stack-version. The decision in all cases makes sense, but it is very easy to reach a bad situation:
I had two stacks deployed:
cassandra 3
cassandra-test 2

by mistake, I've created a new stack:
cassandra test

to fix things, I've deleted:
cassandra-test

so which stock got deleted? unfortunately cassandra-test version 2.
No serious harm done, this time. But I think the tooling should prevent/alert to such issues.

The docs suggest to use the yaml file name from the stack creation, but ".yaml" is not enforced. The fact that aws naming convention is stack-version makes errors more likely.

Best would be, not to allow creation of a confusing ASG - or a stackname that equals a previous stackname+version or a new stackname+version that equals an existing stackname

issue_title: Model validation and unspecified values
issue_body:
Suppose the follwing input. 

    (set-option :rewriter.hi_div0 false)    
    (declare-fun x () (_ BitVec 32))
    (assert (= x (bvudiv (_ bv1 32) (_ bv0 32))))
    ; (push)
    (check-sat)
    (get-value(x))

Currently this produces

    ((x (bvudiv0 #x00000001)))

and all is well. Now switch to incremental mode by uncommenting the `(push)`. It then produces

    ((x #xffffffff))

and the model validation (`model_validate=true`) fails. Adding 

    (set-option :old_simplify.bv.hi_div0 false)

doesn't fix it either, but it does change the result to 

    ((x #x00000000))

and again, model validation fails.

issue_title: Assertion Violation Involving Incremental Bitvector Division by Zero
issue_body:
I'm on `unstable` at commit 09980a496c7b1fbd74c241589011d0cf522dd03c, with a debug + tracing build.  If I run Z3 with the following command line:

```console
z3 rewriter.hi_div0=false old_simplify.bv.hi_div0=false --smt2 test.smt2
```

...where `test.smt2` has the following contents:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_BV)
(declare-fun x0 () (_ BitVec 1))
(check-sat)
(assert (= #b1 (bvudiv #b0 x0)))
(assert (= #b0 (bvudiv x0 #b0)))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

...then Z3 crashes on the `get-model` command with the following output:

```
sat
sat
ASSERTION VIOLATION
File: ../src/sat/sat_solver/inc_sat_solver.cpp
Line: 412
m.is_true(tmp)
(C)ontinue, (A)bort, (S)top, (T)hrow exception, Invoke (G)DB
```

The second call to `check-sat` should result in `unsat`, as any alternative would violate the fact that `bvudiv` is a pure function.  As such, I'm assuming that an invalid model is getting generated internally, and then the fact that it's invalid is later discovered in the `get-model` usage.

While the first use of `check-sat` is basically a no-op in this case, without it Z3 correctly returns that the rest is `unsat`.

issue_title: Implement __copy__ in z3.py
issue_body:
So I think it would be beneficial to implement `__copy__` in z3.py. Basically, I'd want `__copy__` to call `solver.translate`. I was able to make a simple modification on my side to override `__copy__` and it worked as I expected:

```python
In [68]: z3.z3.Solver.__copy__ = lambda self: self.translate(self.ctx)
In [69]: s2 = copy(s)
```

Basically, instead of just creating a link (as it does currently), copy creates an independent copy. If one wants to make a link, they just need to assign a new variable. copy, on the other hand, would be very beneficial.

issue_title: Does lasagne.layers.get_all_params() need some modification?
issue_body:
Right now, lasage.layers.get_all_params() gives a list of all parameters of an individual or list of layers. It works great for almost all cases. But for example, if we work with a network like Faster RCNN or any type of network which basically has a shared convolutional layers initially and branch out (into two) to have multiple FCs (in this case) or any different network. 

So in this case, I only have one FCs in each branch, so I just do a get_all_params(fc1) + [fc2.W,fc.b] to get all the parameters. If the branched out networks are bigger, this obviously would be tougher

Edit 1 - I should have used layer.get_params() instead of writing it's parameters in a list. 

- One way to tackle is just use get_all_params() at all ends of branches (if i may call that) and just **keep unique elements** in the list. Although this seems logically correct, when I print the list of all params I just get W,b,W,b etc so leaving this up to users would be a bad idea? / info on **how to implement** this could be discussed in the thread.
- Other way is to create a new function which will **return params of only the layers between** the shared convs and the end fcs (branches), so basically a subtraction between the lists of fc2 and conv_last (letting this be the last conv layer). Instead of the user manually generating the list of all layers contained in that, what I was thinking was an **automatic** subtraction given two layers and their corresponding parameters **using this function** itself (Referring to the coding convention followed in [this](https://hastebin.com/sovikewiwi.py) manner, where a layer represents everything below it too)

Attached a screenshot to better explain what I've tried to say.

![screenshot from 2016-12-21 01 29 40](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10350518/21365813/0af991e8-c71d-11e6-8eb4-4207c1deec2d.png)

issue_title: Layer normalization for recurrent networks
issue_body:
Hello!
Recently, a new normalization [procedure](https://arxiv.org/abs/1607.06450) has been invented. It provided great results for improving training on both feed-forward and especially recurrent networks.  Training is accelerated several times. It would be great to see it implemented in Lasagne. 

Unfortunately, as I am looking at code for GRU and LSTM it seems to me, that it would be necessary to add another layers like `LN_LSTMLayer` because LN requires to change the step function in `scan` procedure. Also, it seems like it is another use case for [this](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/425) proposal. Also, it is not that hard to change existing layers to choose to use or not use LN.

There is implementation from paper authors. It is not in Lasagne, but rather in Theano.
https://github.com/ryankiros/layer-norm

Waiting for your replies.

 1.9.x  =

```

avoid = (0x10bc,)

issue_title: Truncated lifting on ARM
issue_body:
(Relevant binary attached)
The following block is truncated:
```
addr = p.loader.main_bin.get_symbol('eth_send').addr+0x7c+p.loader.main_bin.rebase_addr
In [11]: p.factory.block(addr).pp()
0x4007ec:	pop	{r3, pc}
```

This block should be:

```
7c:   e8bd8008        pop     {r3, pc}
80:   e3e00000        mvn     r0, #0
84:   e8bd8008        pop     {r3, pc}
```

It is worth nothing that the rest of the block is decoded correctly when skipping the first instruction:

```
In [7]: p.factory.block(addr+4).pp()
0x4007f0:	mvn	r0, #0
0x4007f4:	pop	{r3, pc}
```

At first glance, everything looks fine in terms of size calculation until the call to pyvex.IRSB in simuvex/engines/vex/engine.py so I assume this bug comes from pyvex or below.

[eth.o.gz](https://github.com/angr/simuvex/files/1009358/eth.o.gz)

issue_title: Constrained bytes in files vs. bytes in symbolic variables difference
issue_body:
When a byte in a file is constrained the bit index in the constraint starts at 0, e.g. file[7:0] for the first byte of the file. When a byte in a variable is constrained the bit index starts at VARIABLE_LENGTH-1, e.g. arg[39:32] for a variable that is 40 bits long. This appears to be a recent change. 

I have code that attempts to answer the question is byte X in file/arg constrained. It would help if there was some consistency in how bytes are represented as constraints.

issue_title: App title does not i18n-ed
issue_body:
App titles are always to be capfirst-ed app.label.title().

It should be  {% trans app.name %} that priority of name is

1. verbose_name
2. app.title()

The `verbose_name` is initialized as `app.title()` In django appconfig __init__()  when there is no `verbose_name` specified. It is because we can always use `verbose_name` for  template name.

Problem is located here
https://github.com/django-admin-tools/django-admin-tools/blob/e66125a7cd47dedc92362b2a11ea78b8e7cb7cbe/admin_tools/dashboard/modules.py#L447

It always use `app.title()` instead of `verbose_name`

issue_title: msg_native reader fails when order number has a hyphen in it
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

# Your code here
from satpy import Scene
infile	=	['/indir/MSG2-SEVI-MSG15-0100-NA-20101101135742.336000000Z-20101101135754-1260206-1.nat']
global_scene = Scene(filenames=infile,sensor="seviri", reader="native_msg")

#### Problem description

The above code will fail with an error. If I edit the order number (1260206-1) to something that doesn't contain the hyphen (1260206) then satpy works OK. It appears that the code isn't set up to deal with orders that span multiple parts (-1, -2, etc).
