issue_title: Resampling of swath data - result shape difference
issue_body:
Hello, I'm new to this, but we are looking at using pyresample to speed up our mapping routines of h5 L2 satellite data from polar orbiters. We are currently using GPT provided in BEAM which is java based and very VERY slow.

However, when I tried to use your samples as in the docs:
`#!/usr/bin/env python3

import pyresample as pr
from pyresample import kd_tree, image, bilinear, geometry
import numpy as np

area_def = geometry.AreaDefinition('areaD', 'Europe (3km, HRV, VTC)', 'areaD',{'a': '6378144.0', 'b': '6356759.0','lat_0': '50.00', 'lat_ts': '50.00','lon_0': '8.00', 'proj': 'stere'},800, 800,[-1370912.72, -909968.64,1029087.28, 1490031.36])
data = np.fromfunction(lambda y, x: y*x, (50, 10))
lons = np.fromfunction(lambda y, x: 3 + x, (50, 10))
lats = np.fromfunction(lambda y, x: 75 - y, (50, 10))
swath_def = geometry.SwathDefinition(lons=lons, lats=lats)

#using pyresample.image
swath_con = image.ImageContainerNearest(data, swath_def, radius_of_influence=5000)
area_con = swath_con.resample(area_def)
swath_con_result = area_con.image_data

#using pyresample.kd_tree
resample_nearest_result = kd_tree.resample_nearest(swath_def, data, area_def, radius_of_influence=50000,epsilon=10)
resample_gauss_result = kd_tree.resample_gauss(swath_def, data, area_def, radius_of_influence=50000,sigmas=100)

#using pyresample.bilinear (it appears that the gridding does not take place?
resample_bilinear_result = bilinear.resample_bilinear(data, swath_def, area_def,radius=30000, neighbours=15,nprocs=1, fill_value=0,reduce_data=True, segments=None,epsilon=0)

print("Starting data:")
print("data.min(): ", data.min())
print("data.max(): ", data.max())
print()
print("image.ImageContainerNearest method:")
print("swath_con_result.shape: ", swath_con_result.shape)
print("swath_con_result.size: ", swath_con_result.size)
print("swath_con_result.min(): ",swath_con_result.min())
print("swath_con_result.max(): ",swath_con_result.max())
print()
print("kd_tree.resample_nearest method:")
print("resample_nearest_result.shape: ", resample_nearest_result.shape)
print("resample_nearest_result.size: ", resample_nearest_result.size)
print("resample_nearest_result.min(): ", resample_nearest_result.min())
print("resample_nearest_result.max(): ", resample_nearest_result.max())
print()
print("kd_tree.resample_gauss method:")
print("resample_gauss_result.shape: ", resample_gauss_result.shape)
print("resample_gauss_result.size: ", resample_gauss_result.size)
print("resample_gauss_result.min(): ", resample_gauss_result.min())
print("resample_gauss_result.max(): ", resample_gauss_result.max())
print()
print("bilinear.resample_bilinear method:")
print("resample_bilinear_result.shape: ", resample_bilinear_result.shape)
print("resample_bilinear_result.size: ", resample_bilinear_result.size)
print("resample_bilinear_result.min(): ", resample_bilinear_result.min())
print("resample_bilinear_result.max(): ", resample_bilinear_result.max())`


The results.shape is unexpected for the resample_bilinear_result when I run the resample_test.py file from the code above eg:

./resample_tests.py 
Starting data:
data.min():  0.0
data.max():  441.0

image.ImageContainerNearest method:
swath_con_result.shape:  (800, 800)
swath_con_result.size:  640000
swath_con_result.min():  0.0
swath_con_result.max():  297.0

kd_tree.resample_nearest method:
resample_nearest_result.shape:  (800, 800)
resample_nearest_result.size:  640000
resample_nearest_result.min():  0.0
resample_nearest_result.max():  297.0

kd_tree.resample_gauss method:
resample_gauss_result.shape:  (800, 800)
resample_gauss_result.size:  640000
resample_gauss_result.min():  0.0
resample_gauss_result.max():  297.0

bilinear.resample_bilinear method:
resample_bilinear_result.shape:  (640000, 1)
resample_bilinear_result.size:  640000
resample_bilinear_result.min():  0.0
resample_bilinear_result.max():  0.0

And regardless of what I change in the bilinear.resample_bilinear method, I always get 0 min and max vals and I'm only guessing, but is that because the data isn't gridded at this point?

Thanks,
Brock

issue_title: handle_tick

issue_title: Visual C++ Redistributable Required?
issue_body:
Hi,

We have developed a C#/WPF app which uses the librealsense wrapper. The app references the wrapper, and the installer copies the native realsense2.dll library to the app install directory so the wrapper can find it on a machine which doesn't have the sdk installed. This works fine most of the time, but on some machines we get an "Unable to load DLL" exception when any camera functions are attempted by the app. This problem is immediately fixed when the Visual C++ 2017 Redistributable is installed. Is the redistributable a prerequisite for using librealsense? Interestingly, the Intel.Realsense,Viewer app works ok without the redistributable so I guess maybe it's something to do with the wrapper as the Viewer is C++?

Thanks,
James

issue_title: Small possible divergence with original DQN paper
issue_body:
Hi,

I noticed that there might be a slight difference between this implementation of the network and the original one by DeepMind. Maybe this is a known fact, but I didn't see it mentionned anywhere, and as this implementation seems to try to be as close as possible to the original one, I thought it'd be worth it to point it out.

It boils down to the fact that this implementation uses the default padding from TensorFlow, which is 'VALID', whereas DeepMind didn't document any padding on their convolutional layers.
If we refer to the Torch implementation they released, we can conclude that they used the default padding of Torch (which is 0 in the SpatialConvolution module), except for the first layer, where they used padding=1

After the convolutions, the image sizes are quite different: 7x7 for (py)Torch and 11x11 for TensorFlow (40% difference). As such, the input size of the first linear layer diverge (3136 vs 7744)

I'm not sure that makes a huge difference (be it positive or negative) in the outcome, but experience has proved that devil's in the details when it comes to deep architectures.

What do you guys think ?

issue_body:
According to the docs args are optional by default: 

```python
class RechargeSim(graphene.Mutation):
    class Arguments:
        msisdn = graphene.String(required=True)
        network_id = graphene.String(required=True)
        product_id = graphene.String()
        airtime_amount = graphene.Float()
```

Here is a query:
```graphql
           result = authed_graphql_client.execute(
                '''
                mutation($msisdn: String!, $networkId: String!, $productId: String!) {
                  rechargeSim(msisdn: $msisdn,
                              networkId: $networkId,
                              productId: $productId) {
                    rechargeId
                    message
                  }
                }
                ''',
                variable_values={
                    'msisdn': sim.msisdn,
                    'networkId': network_id,
                    'productId': product_id
                }
            )
```

I get this error: 
`TypeError: mutate() missing 1 required positional argument: 'airtime_amount'  `

I have tried the following variations:

```python
class RechargeSim(graphene.Mutation):
    class Arguments:
        msisdn = graphene.String(required=True)
        network_id = graphene.String(required=True)
        product_id = graphene.String()
        airtime_amount = graphene.Float(required=False)```

class RechargeSim(graphene.Mutation):
    class Arguments:
        msisdn = graphene.String(required=True)
        network_id = graphene.String(required=True)
        product_id = graphene.String()
        airtime_amount = graphene.Float(default_value=None)```

class RechargeSim(graphene.Mutation):
    class Arguments:
        msisdn = graphene.String(required=True)
        network_id = graphene.String(required=True)
        product_id = graphene.String()
        airtime_amount = graphene.Float(required=False, default_value=None)```
```

In the end I have hacked around this by defaulting the value to 0 and then resetting it to None in the resolver if 0.

issue_title: Use UUIDs instead of Relay IDs
issue_body:
Hey Guys,

Awesome library 👍 
We are looking for some best practises regarding the use of Relay IDs together with UUIDs.

**Our Setup**
We are using Django Graphene with two different setups:
1. Redux & GraphQL (no Relay)
2. Relay

We are using a postgresql for DB. Our django models generate UUIDs for all our entities. We really like the UUIDs :)

Based on the following issue https://github.com/facebook/relay/issues/191 and the fact that we want the client to use a single ID throughout the app, we have decided that the client side should only know about the Relay ID. 

**The Problem**

This raises three issues:
1.  Mutations and advanced resolves with foreign keys need to be translated from Relay IDs to our UUIDs. 
2. More importantly, it makes it much harder to debug ids all the way from client to server.
3. It makes it impossible to use non graphql APIs.

**Current Solution**
We have written a middleware that solves problem (1), by translating IDs to our UUIDs. 
However, this does not solve (2) and (3), and we still feel our solution is kind of patchy :)  

**Questions**
1. Can we use our UUIDs instead of the base64 IDs generated by graphene? Would it still work fine with Relay?
2.Any other suggestions or other best practises we should use?

Thanks!
Nir

issue_title: Library functions are extracted into auxiliary.
issue_body:
The graph explorer currently extracts function library nodes into the auxiliary scene group when a user adds or removes a node to / from the core graph.

This is because the graph explorer deems any node that lacks inputs or outputs to be extracted, and the library functions lack inputs and outputs because they serve as templates for other ops. The library functions should not be in the core graph because they do not represent ops in the graph.

issue_title: Joiner does not give correct priority to available networks
issue_body:
Currently, the joiner (joiner.cpp) gives priority to networks on higher channels. This has the effect that a joiner can accidentally fail to join the correct network if there is another network on a higher channel which is listing 'accept any' steering data. The Thread spec 1.1.1 section 8.4.4.1.2 specifies a priority system for choosing a network to try and join.

I will shortly submit a pull request to make sure that the highest priority network is selected for joining rather than the highest channel.

I'm not sure how to implement the queue for networks that is described in a way that fits with the rest of openthread. Statically allocating a space in memory seems messy and wasteful given that joining is rare and only when not any other thread role. Message allocation is obviosly not intended for something like this. Any advice?

issue_title: Avoid multiple validation
issue_body:
Currently we end up validating structs multiple times. All the fields of a struct are validated upon construction. But if the struct is then used as an argument to another struct, all fields on that new struct will be validated -- including the already-validated struct. This can be really expensive when a large number of objects is involved, and I wouldn't be surprised if this is significantly hurting our deserialization performance.

```
foos = [Foo(x) for i in xrange(1000)]  # all objects are validated upon construction
bar = Bar(foos=foos)  # here they're validated again
```

One possible solution is to track this in a `_validated` attribute. It'll have to be set to False when attributes on the object are changed manually using `obj.attr = foo`.

issue_title: Manage debug mode with more precision
issue_body:
For now, we permit to display or not a traceback when context is in debug mode. We should permit more flexible:

When add exception handling with a decorator, or directly with context:

* Permit to allow or not a message extracted from exception.

By default, behavior should be: Not in debug mode, and no precision about message: no message
When debug mode is activated: extract message from exception. When no debug mode but allow message from exception: extract message from exception.

This is important to permit to not expose internal sensitive informations when error.

issue_title: Can't set batch size in training to 'None' when using layers.estimator.regression
issue_body:
The default batch size in tflearn.layers.estimator.regression is set to 64 and _must_ be an integer. If I try and set it to None then it throws up an error. Whereas in model.fit(), you can specify batch_size=None, but this doesn't seem to work and you end up with a batch_size of 64. 

I can get around this by simply setting the batch_size to the number of training examples (in my case 3300) and it will work.

However, if you now want to introduce a reshape layer, then you have to specify the batch size in the new shape (you can't specify 'None' in the reshape function for the batch_size as the inputs must be an integer). This is fine when training, but produces an error when using the validation data, as there is a different number of examples (in my case 350). 

Overall, the issue stems from not being above to specify a batch size of 'None' in the regression function.

issue_title: Switch from `setattr` to decorators?
issue_body:
I notice in `__init__.py`, you use `setattr` metaprogramming to make `Visdom` methods work on both Torch tensors and numpy arrays. This seems to be confusing static analysis with Jedi, causing autocomplete for `Visdom` methods to fail in various IDEs. 

How would you feel about switching to using a decorator for methods that  take can a tensor or array instead of `setattr`? That would be more transparent to static analysis tools and restore autocomplete. Arguably it's also cleaner, since the annotation that a method works on tensors and arrays would be inline with the method instead of that logic happening in a seperate block. 

I can submit a PR for discussion.

issue_title: Support transfer of ownership of Python subclasses of pybind11 C++ classes with `shared_ptr<>`
issue_body:
If a Python subclass of a pybind11 C++ class, with `holder_type = shared_ptr<T>`, has a `shared_ptr<>` that exists in C++ when its Python `ref_count()` goes to zero, Python will destroy the Python extension, and leave the C++ Alias type (e.g. `PyBase`, using `PYBIND11_OVERLOAD*`) functionality bare (and actually aliased / truncated).

This could be prevented if a custom `__del__`, `PyTypeObject::tp_del`, or `PyTypeObject::tp_dealloc` is installed such that the instance could be resurrected if `holder.use_count() > 1` for the given value-holder, and the ownership is released to C++.
(`__del__` is the easiest, as Python explicitly mentions that you can increase the refcount of a destructing object and save it from death.)

This is related to #1132.

issue_title: Unable to get name of derived class on C++ side
FindPythonLibsNew.cmake not working with MSYS Python interpreter
issue_body:
FindPythonLibsNew.cmake tests for windows system (CMAKE_HOST_WIN32 is true) then it assumes that the Python interpreter works with windows-like directories hierarchy (lines 141-143).
However, it does not work when using MSYS' python interpreter which assumes a Unix-like directories hierarchy.
Note that compiling python bindings with MSYS' mingw compiler requires to use MSYS' python interpreter. For instance, anaconda's interpreter's include files do not need to be compatible with MSYS' compiler due to version mismatch.

issue_title: Deadlock when sub-interpreter throws exception
Eventual collisions between opaque STL containers
issue_body:
After some chatting on gitter, @jagerman suggested that I raise an issue here, mostly so that it can be kept track of.

This is related to #439, but has more to do with what happens in the long run when more and more packages are developed using pybind11.

Currently,  opaque STL containers are most often generated with a combo of the `PYBIND11_MAKE_OPAQUE` macro and a call to `py::bind_vector`.

If two different modules by different developers generate the same opaque containers, an import error occurs if both modules are used together.  One simple solution is to wrap the binding in try/catch blocks as shown here: https://github.com/molpopgen/opaque_test.  (That example uses ccpimport to compile the code.)

However, the try/catch solution is imperfect: the name seen on the Python side depends on the import order.  Further, there is the deeper problem that `py::bind_vector` may optionally be invoked with `py::buffer_protocol()` and that the class type returned may be further augmented via `.defs` in the usual way.  The possible result then is that both the name and features/behavior depend on import order, which seems less than ideal.

The gitter discussion ended with the agreement that a solution is needed and that it is not clear what that solution should look like.

issue_title: Binded function default arguments only evaluated once
ssl and token issue with from_service_account
issue_body:
Hi,

Found two issues with pykube.KubeConfig.from_service_account.

1) Cannot turn SSL verification off. 

There has to be a way to either specify the verify flag when calling the pykube api or set the verify to False in HttpClient.build_session() when no "certificate-authority" is found in self.config.cluster

2) The token read in KubeConfig.from_service_account appends a newline at the end of the token making it an invalid header value. 

This can be fixed with: token = fp.read().rstrip('\n')

Thanks,
Sudeep

issue_title: talisker > 0.6.7 does not work with celery 3.1.23
issue_body:
We had deployed delta-service with talisker 0.6.7 and celery 3.1.23. This combination was working fine. Upon upgrading to talisker 0.9.0, we can now no longer queue tasks:

https://sentry.ols.staging.canonical.com/canonical/delta-service/issues/43/

The version of celery has not changed. Downgrading talisker to 0.6.7 fixes the problem. I realise this is super odd - the stack trace is entirely inside celery itself... but perhaps talisker patches some aspect of celery?

I downgraded talisker on stg, and verified that everything started working again. Then upgraded again, and verified that it broke again. The only package that changed was talisker. 

celery 4.0 is out now, but the talisker docs do not mention whether 4.x is supported - I'm n ot sure if upgrading celery *and* talisker will help (and I wanted to avoid too many simultaneously moving parts).

issue_title: https redirect don't work for subdomains using apache plugin
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 16.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
The ppa certbot/certbot

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
$ sudo certbot --apache

I got the certficates for my domain: example.com.br and the subdomain edu.example.com.br

When asked if I wanted to reddirect http resquests to the https site I confirmed for both sites, the script then exit gracefully.

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
When acessing http://example.com.br it reddirects me to https://example.com.br as expected.
But when I acess http://edu.example.com.br it doesn't reddirect to https://edu.example.com.br.

The script modify files on /etc/apache2/sites-enabled for both domains, but the redirect rule is the same.
On the virtualhost for the edu.example.com.br domain, I have to modify this createad by the script:

RewriteCond %{SERVER_NAME} =example.com.br

issue_title: -v should imply --text
issue_body: If you specify the -v flag without --text, the verbose logging gets sent into an ncurses text box, and is impossible to read. We should automatically assume --text when -v is specified.

issue_title: Allow user interaction in manual plugin hooks
issue_body: Users should be able to write interactive hooks for manual authenticator. Certbot is currently running the hook script and displaying the output afterwards, interactive prompts just halt the process.

issue_title: Renewal: Preserve --oscp-must-staple
issue_body:
### Description:

Certbot creates a certificate with `OSCP Must Staple` extension when called with option `--must-staple`.

However, the option `--must-staple` is not stored in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/www.example.com.conf`. In my case:

```
[renewalparams]
installer = apache
authenticator = apache
rsa_key_size = 4096
account = abc
```

There also seems not to be an entry for `OSCP Must Staple` in the [test file](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/tests/testdata/sample-renewal.conf#L8).

When executing `certbot renew` for renewal, certbot renews certificates without `OSCP Must Staple`, respectively.

### Suggested Fix:

- For renewal, certbot should be aware that the old certificate was generated with  `--must-staple`. That information should be stored in the `[renewalparams]` section, respectively.
- For `certbot renew`, the new certificate should be generated with `--must-staple`, in case the old certificate was generated with that extension.

### Use case:

- I have some certificates which I want to renew with `--must-staple`, because e.g. apache supports that.
- I have other certificates which I use with other software (e.g. an ancient mailserver) which does not support OSCP at all.
- For `certbot renew`, certbot should be aware which one is which, using `[renewalparams]`. Otherwise, I'd have to generate certificates otherwise again (e.g. manually), which basically doesn't allow me to make use of `certbot renew`.

### Version:
```
certbot --version
certbot 0.9.3
```

OS: Debian 8, with certbot installed via official debian repository.

issue_title: ErrorHandler causing errors
issue_body: Well, sort of. The `ErrorHandler` class adds its own signal handlers to those listed in `_SIGNALS` at the top of the file. Some of these signals seem to be ignored by default on some systems (such as SIGXFSZ on Ubuntu 16.04). Regardless of them being ignored, we still install our own signal handlers that cause the program to exit/run error handling code. When setting signal handlers, we should only modify those that aren't being ignored by checking if `signal.getsignal` returns `signal.SIG_IGN`.

issue_title: Documentation sidebar not scrollable
issue_body: The left navigation sidebar in the official docs (https://certbot.eff.org/docs/) is not scrollable leading to inability to access all topics on small screens. The mirror on ReadTheDocs functions correctly so it appears to be a styling / CSS problem.

issue_title: Allow requesting wildcard certificates in the client
issue_body:
The ACME spec allows for the possibility of wildcard certificates. Certbot is an ACME client that should be able to be used by any ACME server so, as suggested by @terribleplan, we should try to allow users to request wildcard certificates as well.

There's a number of things to consider when doing this, however. First, since Let's Encrypt is currently the only ACME server, all requests for wildcard certificates will fail. With this in mind, we should make sure that the error message given to the user when they request wildcard certificates is reasonable. Ideally, this is done by showing the error message returned by Let's Encrypt. If that doesn't work, we can either add our own warning message or own error checking when the server is Let's Encrypt.

issue_title: Play nicely with containers
issue_body:
Got the following feedback from someone who had tried to use certbot, but couldn't due to complications:

there's nothing specifically wrong with let's encrypt, it's more of a function of our infrastructure. in order to validate our domain with let's encrypt, we need to periodically (every ~3 months) pass an acme challenge by hosting a file they give us. this is relatively harder when using kubernetes, or any other container orchestration framework, because containers are scheduled across a variety of nodes depending on their resource constraints. each container usually does only performs a single role (e.g. run the web service, run nginx, run a single background job, etc.), so it's not as easy as just adding a cron job like the documentation says

one option is to spin up another container whose job it is to update the cert periodically, serve the acme challenge on some local server, link it to the nginx container, and have nginx proxy for that local server

or share a filesystem mount between the two containers, but that could be problematic because containers are separated across hosts (though kubernetes specifically does have a good abstraction for this)

another option is to directly integrate with nginx or haproxy by using their scripting extensions. for instance, nginx has the lua open resty module that lets you dynamically request certs and cache them within nginx, which is super nice because it doesn't involve any additional coordination between containers or host mounts

but there's no standard open resty image that does that for you easily. I spent a good few days writing and configuring one for my personal websites, and got it working, but imo it's not worth the effort right now

and that's really the ultimate issue with all of this - it's definitely possible, but it takes a good amount of time, and the other option is clicking a few buttons and getting a cert for 1 year or longer
so to let us focus on development and not ssl configuration, just buying a cert at this stage for 1 or more years makes more sense imo

questions:
does the plugin automatically request and renew certs for virtual hosts? will they be providing a docker image that has that functionality built it? does nginx need to be compiled with certain extensions (e.g. lua) to work with that functionality? how does it persist the certs? will the certs get cycled every time containers are restarted or rescheduled?

issue_title: Wildcard Certificates Support
issue_body:
I have a question regarding the new support in `Let's Encrypt` for wildcard certificates. As [they announced](https://letsencrypt.org/2017/07/06/wildcard-certificates-coming-jan-2018.html).

However, they also say the following.

> Let’s Encrypt will begin issuing wildcard certificates in January of 2018. Wildcard certificates are a commonly requested feature and we understand that there are some use cases where they make HTTPS deployment easier. I would like to know if `cerbot` is already is prepared for this, and if so, is there a way to test it?

issue_title: The audience argument for @endpoints.api should be optional
issue_body:
The [documentation](https://cloud.google.com/endpoints/docs/frameworks/python/troubleshoot-jwt) states the following:

> If the `"aud"` (audience) claim in a JWT token matches the Cloud Endpoints service name, then Endpoints Frameworks validates the audience and ignores the values set in the `audiences` argument in the `@endpoints.api` decorator. For example, if your service name is `"myservice.appspot.com"`, then a JWT with `"aud"` set to `"myservice.appspot.com"` or `"https://myservice.appspot.com"` is a valid audience.

That doesn't seem to work.
Following an example described on [this](https://cloud.google.com/endpoints/docs/openapi/service-account-authentication) page, I generate a Google ID token and set `TARGET_AUD` with `https://[SERVICE_NAME]`, where `[SERVICE_NAME]` is the value of the `host` entry in the API configuration file. Then I expect the `audience` argument to be ignored as it's stated in the docs but it's actually still required.
The interesting thing is that it's optional and works as expected when authentication arguments are configured in `@endpoints.method`. That could be related to another bug (#64) that exposes differences between `@endpoints.api` and `@endpoints.method`.

issue_title: Make `smoketest` configurable to allow for other ethereum client backends
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The implementation of the `smoketest` command tries to allow to specify other ethereum client backends via environment variables. The implementation is however not complete:
- the command for running the ethereum client backend is configurable via `RST_GETH_BINARY`
- the init phase with the smoketest genesis is **not** configurable.

Therefore, it is not possible to use an ethereum client for the smoketest, that does not follow `geth` s CLI spec for `init`.

## Solution

Either remove the customization code to make the existing smoketest implementation simpler, or add a scheme that allows to also define the initialization phase with the smoketest genesis JSON file for other clients.

issue_title: Handle potential race with blocknumber in log_events()
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

At the moment in [`transaction_log.log_events()`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a525609403bcc20c453de9e15b713064336a51e9/raiden/raiden_service.py#L1344) we get the block number by using `get_block_number()` function from the raiden service.

By the time we reach the logging stage a new block could have been mined and we would be using that instead of the actual block in which the event occured.

## Solution

Instead of just adding the block number only at the time of logging, make sure that each task knows the current block number and its value is updated by the Block state change. That can be achieved in one of 2 ways:

1. Adding the block_number as an attribute to all events generated by state change transitions
2. Passing the block_number around by wrapping it around the event as they are.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Make sure each task knows the current block number.

issue_title: Implement NAT punching/port mapping methods
issue_body:
Currently users have to configure their NAT manually and configure the port mapping for the world facing listen address (i.e. the forwarded port at the router + the external IP address) manually by giving the `--external-listen-address` parameter. Ideally this should never be necessary and raiden should figure out how to be accessible to the world by itself.

A start for this has been made with the [UPnP `nat.py` module](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/nat.py), but there should be other methods implemented as well, for situations where UPnP is not available.

See also #118

issue_title: Out of order transfers with off by one nonce
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

During our testing of raiden in the testnet we have frequently noticed out of order transfers with off by one nonces appearing. The log looks like this:

```
INFO:raiden.network.protocol    MESSAGE RECEIVED node:9bed7fd1 echohash:63c03ce4 <MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>

WARNING:raiden.channel.netting_channel  Received out of order transfer from 4f8904a0. Expected nonce: 6480805001953281 but got nonce: 6480805001953280                                                                                                                          
CRITICAL:raiden.network.protocol        maybe unwanted transfer e=<MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                
DEBUG:raiden.network.protocol   <MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                                                  
INFO:raiden.network.protocol    MESSAGE RECEIVED node:9bed7fd1 echohash:63c03ce4 <MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                 
INFO:raiden.message_handler     message received message=<MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                         
WARNING:raiden.channel.netting_channel  Received out of order transfer from 4f8904a0. Expected nonce: 6480805001953281 but got nonce: 6480805001953280                                                                                                                          
CRITICAL:raiden.network.protocol        maybe unwanted transfer e=<MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                
DEBUG:raiden.network.protocol   <MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                                                  
DEBUG:raiden.tasks      new block timestamp=1503070769.64 number=1509609                                                                                                                                                                                                        
INFO:raiden.network.protocol    MESSAGE RECEIVED node:9bed7fd1 echohash:63c03ce4 <MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                 
INFO:raiden.message_handler     message received message=<MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                         
WARNING:raiden.channel.netting_channel  Received out of order transfer from 4f8904a0. Expected nonce: 6480805001953281 but got nonce: 6480805001953280                                                                                                                          
CRITICAL:raiden.network.protocol        maybe unwanted transfer e=<MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                
DEBUG:raiden.network.protocol   <MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                                                  
INFO:raiden.network.protocol    MESSAGE RECEIVED node:9bed7fd1 echohash:63c03ce4 <MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                 
INFO:raiden.message_handler     message received message=<MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>                                                                                         
WARNING:raiden.channel.netting_channel  Received out of order transfer from 4f8904a0. Expected nonce: 6480805001953281 but got nonce: 6480805001953280                                                                                                                          
CRITICAL:raiden.network.protocol        maybe unwanted transfer e=<MediatedTransfer [token:0f114a1e nonce:6480805001953280 transferred_amount:0 lock_amount:4 hash:3471d388 locksroot:66d90568]>
```
We are node  `9bed7fd1` and we received a mediated transfer from `4f8904a0` which is out of order because of the nonce. The other node (`4f8904a0`) keeps repeating the transfer ad infinitum, so the above log just keeps repeating.

Our node probably has received the message with nonce `6480805001953280`, processed it and is now waiting for `6480805001953281` from the partner. That never comes because the partner still thinks we are at nonce `6480805001953280`.

We need to investigate and find out why this happens.

Possible reasons this happens are:

1. Our node does not correctly send the ACK for processing the message with nonce `6480805001953280` to the partner.
2. The partner does not receive that ACK, or does not process it correctly.
3. There is some kind of race condition on the value of the transfer's nonce.


## Solution

Find out why this happens, consistently reproduce it, write a test if possible and then solve it.

issue_title: Result mismatch problem with inconsistent use of `_index` and `_output`
issue_body:
Currently I cannot provide an MWE for this one, but when I rerun my workflow with `-s force` SoS keeps aborting the runs with `result-mismatch` error message:

```
INFO: 8f61d0e6b6be7a86d14124ca0950a8a7 re-execute completed
INFO: 8f61d0e6b6be7a86d14124ca0950a8a7 queued
INFO: 228040a86fa69b8875132ccb5cbcce09 restart from status result-mismatch
INFO: 228040a86fa69b8875132ccb5cbcce09 queued
INFO: 228040a86fa69b8875132ccb5cbcce09 started
INFO: 8f61d0e6b6be7a86d14124ca0950a8a7 started
INFO: 228040a86fa69b8875132ccb5cbcce09 completed
INFO: 8f61d0e6b6be7a86d14124ca0950a8a7 completed
WARNING: 228040a86fa69b8875132ccb5cbcce09 result-mismatch
ERROR: 1 job completed, 1 job result mismatch
```

Intuitively, I think for `-s force` since I'm specifically asking it to rerun everything regardless of whatever exists, should I really expect any `result mismatch`? When I run with `-v4` I see lines like this:

```
DEBUG: result-mismatch: 617 completed: 906 aborted: 131 failed: 198 pending: 4 running: 2
```
which could be a bit confusing because in my workflow there should be only a couple of tasks. (I understand these lines may refer to runs from other workflows). 

BTW it may also worth mentioning that if I rerun when I first fail with `-s force` then the problem goes away. However another task in the workflow may start complaining the same. If I run many times, then the problem will get "fixed" one at a time for each run and eventually I'm able to execute the entire workflow without an issue. Not sure if these descriptions are enough to pin the problem.

issue_title: A scratch panel for SoS jupyter frontend
issue_body:
The nbextension [scratchpad](https://github.com/minrk/nbextension-scratchpad) is very useful in that it allows us to test commands, check variables, etc without saving things to the notebook. Unfortunately, the scratchpad does not work well with SoS frontend because its panel take the right half of the window and covers the kernel-selection buttons.

I have submitted [a request](https://github.com/minrk/nbextension-scratchpad/issues/11) to make the scratchpad panel float so that we can move the panel to the left. We can also consider adding a scratchpad to SoS, with

1. A toolbar button (instead of floating like the current scratchpad extension) to open/close scratch pad, in a way similar to toc2.
2. The scratchpad can be float, but would by default dock at the left similar to toc2. 
3. Execution of statements in scratchpad would not increase `execution_count`.

Implementation wise we can borrow ideas from [scratchpad](https://github.com/minrk/nbextension-scratchpad) (execution of cells) and [toc2](https://github.com/ipython-contrib/jupyter_contrib_nbextensions/tree/master/src/jupyter_contrib_nbextensions/nbextensions/toc2) (UI).

I do think this can be a good addition to our SoS frontend but let us wait and see if @minrk can make the scratchpad float.

issue_title: task signature mode
issue_body:
It is not entirely clear how to use task signature. In the new branch, task signatures and status are saved in one file. The problem is that

1. `sos execute` will ignore the task if it is completed.
2. `sos execute -s force` will ignore the task signature

So in both case the saved signatures are not used.

We could allow `sos execute` to run, but it will be a change of behavior and cause rerun of tasks if no signature is used (e.g. no `output` is specified).

issue_title: Clarify docs that doctr is not only for sphinx
Using a field representing real numbers with the iterator
issue_body:
I am trying to learn a regressor on text data and I use torchtext in all my other tasks but I see a problem in using it for this use case.

I define the field for targets as follows:
```
TARGETS = data.Field(
            sequential=False, tensor_type=torch.DoubleTensor, batch_first=True)
self.fields = [('targets', TARGETS), ('text', TEXT)]
self.train, self.val, self.test = data.TabularDataset.splits(
            path=self.path,
            train=self.train_suffix,
            validation=self.val_suffix,
            test=self.test_suffix,
            format=formatting,
            fields=self.fields)
TEXT.build_vocab(self.train)
```

I have a file that contains tab separate <values>\t<text>

When I make iterators out of it, 
```
train_iter, val_iter, test_iter = data.Iterator.splits(
                (self.train, self.val, self.test),
                batch_sizes=(self.batch_size, self.test_batch_size,
                             self.test_batch_size),
                sort_key=lambda x: len(x.text),
                shuffle=True)
print(next(iter(train_iter)))
```
it gives me an error when getting the next batch:

> AttributeError: 'Field' object has no attribute 'vocab'

I know this is because I didn't run .build_vocab for the TARGETS field. But why do I really need to do this? What if I just want to get real numbers and compute losses on them? 

Any workaround is appreciated. If I am doing something wrong, please let me know too.

issue_title: transform.ToPILImage does not work properly with RGBA
issue_body:
The transform `ToPILImage` seems to force RGB when converting from an ndarray. Here's an example: ```` import numpy as np import torchvision from torchvision import transforms from skimage import io toPil = transforms.ToPILImage() image = io.imread('rand.png') print (image.shape) print (np.asarray(toPil(image)).shape) ```` `print (image.shape) ` returns (320, 480, 4), `print (np.asarray(toPil(image)).shape) ` returns (320, 480, 3), I made sure the image works with PIL: ```` from PIL import Image import torchvision from torchvision import transforms toPil = transforms.ToPILImage() toTens= transforms.ToTensor() image = Image.open('rand.png') print (image) print(toPil(toTens(image))) ```` `print (image)` returns
`print(toPil(toTens(image)))` returns
Visualizing the image makes it very clear that there's something wrong. I don't think this is intended behavior. Can anyone confirm this?

issue_title: Provided pretrained feature extractors also (removing final pooling and fully connected layers)
issue_body:
It would be very helpful if apart from providing pretrained networks torchvision packages also provide pretrained feature extractors (that is the learnt convolutions but not the pooling and fully connected layer). 

For transfer learning one only needs the convolution maps and it is [somewhat cumbersome](https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/given-vgg16-how-to-remove-pool5-layer-and-all-the-classify-layers-and-how-to-add-new-layers-to-that-while-keeping-the-pretrained-weights/1653/7) to manually remove the fully connected and convolutions. 
Also since resnet does not contain sequential layer as well so we would need to manually copy all layers one by one for extracting learnt weights. 
The average pooling at end also doesn't allow image sizes less than 224x224 (though we can use transfer learning for smaller image sizes as well!)

It seems pretty useful to me, what are your opinions

issue_title: Using plasma on Kubernetes
issue_body:
I am trying to use plasma to store datasets in memory, and share them between pods.

I find that this does not work well, and in particular, plasma.get/plasma.put tends to hang with no specific error message.

I am sure other people have tried this setup, I would love to hear about their experience.

The setup is:
- Running plasma_store and clients in different pod on the same node
- The socket is placed in one R/W volume mounted in all pods
- Tried plasma_store using shm and hugepages (`-h`). shm hangs if `get()`ing an object submitted by another client, hugepages seems to complain about missing huge pages when mmapping

Note that I could get this running using Docker containers just fine. I understand that some of those issues are due to Kubernetes more than plasma, but I would love some pointers.

cc @mitar

issue_title: Sca
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.
-->

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Linux Ubuntu 16.04 
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: pip
- **Ray version**: 0.4.0
- **Python version**: 3.5.2
- **Exact command to reproduce**:

<!--
You can obtain the Ray version with

python -c "import ray; print(ray.__version__)"
-->

### Describe the problem
<!-- Describe the problem clearly here. -->
My server has 64 cores, so I would assume it can run at least 60 actors (remote network) in parallel.

I am modifying the example at [http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/using-ray-with-tensorflow.html](url) to try weight averaging. It seems that scalability is not quite as expected. With more actors (networks) created, the step() function of actors takes longer to finish. I would assume its running time should not increase significantly if not flat. In my case, with one worker, it takes 0.17s; with 20 workers, it takes 1.9s; with 60 workers, it takes 3.8s. Increase is quite big.

It is quite different when I experimented with a pure independent function (not a class/actor function). The running time of the function does not increase much, but the get() time on remote copies of the function increases quite significantly. For example, the running time of the function stays around 3.5s, 
but the get() time increases from 3.5s of single worker to 13.5s of 60 workers, not sure where the 10s gap comes from.

I really appreciate if you could help me fix the scalability problem of multiple networks (actors).

Thanks for your help!
### Source code / logs
<!-- Include any logs or source code that would be helpful to diagnose the problem. If including tracebacks, please include the full traceback. Large logs and files should be attached. Try to provide a reproducible test case that is the bare minimum necessary to generate the problem. -->

issue_title: Some feedback?
issue_body:
Just checked this out with little context as it was mentioned at JypyterCon.
This looks amazing!

Two things in the interface that surprised me:
If I click on the unstaged or staged files, I see the full file, not the diff.
On the other hand, if I go back to the last commit, the interface will just show me the diff, and I don't see a way to view the full notebook.
I think it would be good to be able to view both the diff and the full notebook snapshot, both for earlier commits and for the unstaged and staged files. Having different behavior for commits and the current state was a bit surprising.

The other thing I was surprised by (which I imagine is on your todo) is that the diff is just a git diff of the json, and not an nbdime style diff.

Maybe I would show the diff by default, both for commits and the staging area, and allow viewing the full file via the context menu (looks like there is no context menu yet).

Keep up the good work!

issue_title: Quickly Determine Extents of a Product
issue_body:
It is extremely slow to query the full available extents for a large Product in a Data Cube Index.

This has been frustrating for several developers, and has led to multiple implementations of precomputing and caching this information. Details in the code of each of the following projects:

   - [DEA Dashboard (cubedash)](https://github.com/data-cube/dea-dashboard)
   - [Data Cube Web Map Service](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-wms)
   - [WCS Server](https://github.com/ceos-seo/django-datacube-wcs)

This slowness also affects the pre-flight job tasking which is done 

A simple solution would be an extra database table containing extents and stats about each product, with an offline job which is run regularly to keep it up to date.

The easiest way to implement this would be to take the code from one of the above projects, and include it in `datacube-core`, along with a command to update the table.

## Outstanding Questions
* Can the code from one of these projects be used almost directly, or will new code be required?
* Should the new table store a simple bounding box, or use PostGIS powers to calculate a more accurate vector extent?
* We don't have a robust _migrations_ system for upgrade the Database Schema, it's handled by some bespoke code. Is it worth improving this at the same time, or hacking in something which runs automatically, or having a manually run command to enable this *Extents* extension.

issue_title: find_datasets_lazy isn't actually lazy
issue_body:
### Expected behaviour

- Expect datasets to stream out of DB, first dataset arriving way before query completes
- Expect memory usage to be bounded no matter the size of the result set

### Actual behaviour

- Memory usage is proportional to result set
- The entire response from DB is read and cached in memory, before first dataset becomes available
- Only construction of `Dataset` objects from DB response is happening in a "lazy" fashion

issue_title: Gracefully shutting down nurseries on KeyboardInterrupt
issue_body:
I am experimenting with nested nurseries to handle multiple tasks associated with each incoming connection.  I would like to send a final message on each connection as the nursery exits.  I've run some tests and noticed a few things that seem to make this difficult.

1) KeyboardInterrupt is only raised on the outer Nursery (at which point the nested nurseries, tasks, and incoming sockets have been dismantled)
2) ```KeyboardInterrupt``` triggers a ```trio.Cancelled``` in tasks (which can be caught) but not in children nursery scopes.  If we push enough state down into the tasks this might be workable, but it would be easier to handle at the parent/nursery level.
3) ```finally``` seems the only way to trigger code as we leave a child nursery but as soon as we await, we exit (presumably because the scope has been cancelled)

What is the best way to trigger "awaitable" code (eg send one last message) as we exit a child nursery from a KeyboardInterrupt?

The following code illustrates the issue (without using any networking :-).  I would like to trigger my shutdown code in the "inner" scope:
```
import trio

async def print_message(msg):
	try:
		while True:
			print(msg)
			await trio.sleep(1)
	except trio.Cancelled:
		print("Cancelled Print Message")
		await trio.sleep(1)
		print("Cancelled Blocking Print Message")
	finally:
		print("Finally Print Message")
		await trio.sleep(1)
		print("Finally Blocking Print Message")

async def print_spawner(nursery):
	count = 0
	while True:
		nursery.spawn(print_message, "msg {}".format(count))
		count += 1
		await trio.sleep(1)

async def child():
	try:
		async with trio.open_nursery() as inner_nursery:
			inner_nursery.spawn(print_spawner, inner_nursery)
	except KeyboardInterrupt:
		print("Inner Keyboard")
	except trio.Cancelled:
		print("Inner Cancelled")
		await trio.sleep(1)
		print("Inner Blocking Cancelled")
	finally:
		print("Finally Inner")
		await trio.sleep(1)
		print("Finally Blocking Inner")
	print("Inner Nursery Finished")


async def parent():
	try:
		async with trio.open_nursery() as outer_nursery:
			outer_nursery.spawn(child)
	except KeyboardInterrupt:
		print("Outer Keyboard")
	except trio.Cancelled:
		print("Outer Cancelled")
		await trio.sleep(1)
		print("Outer Blocking Cancelled")
	finally:
		print("Finally Outer")
		await trio.sleep(1)
		print("Finally Blocking Outer")
	print("Outer Nursery Finished")

trio.run(parent)
```
```
msg 0
msg 0
msg 1
^CCancelled Print Message
Cancelled Print Message
Finally Print Message
Finally Print Message
Finally Inner
Outer Keyboard
Finally Outer
Finally Blocking Outer
Outer Nursery Finished
```

issue_title: Handling fields that may or may not contain relationship information
issue_body:
I'm not even sure how to pose this question.

I have data like:

```
Dave's Credit Recovery / Assignee / Ed's Tire Shop
Eads Asset Collection as assignee of GASCO
GASCO, LLC
GiantBank as Successor Of 3rd National Slightly Less Giant Bank
```
Dedupe's working great for clustering `Dave's Credit Recovery / Assignee / ANY ASSIGNEE` correctly. I imagine I can train it to do the opposite as well, though I haven't tried. 

But is there any way to get Dedupe to also parse and split the relationship in the text field? It doesn't feel entirely far off from the FuzzyCategorical variable type, but that's not quite right for this job

If this is beyond the scope of dedupe, would you have any recommendations for pre-processing that can handle hundreds/thousands of variations of a core set of relationships that are (sometimes) present in the text string? 

Another approach that I think could work would be training the system twice -- once for the "left side" and once for the "right side" of the relationship. As far as I can tell, the order is always consistent, even if the string expressing the relationship varies in form.

issue_title: Is there a proper way to add learnable activation functions to convolution layers?
issue_body:
Suppose I have a custom activation function `cust_act()` that has a parameter `alpha` that is learnable. Now I want to apply it to a convolution layer as below:
`y = Conv2d(x, 1, ..., act=cust_act)`

I see that the parameter `alpha` does not add to the `y.all_params` list and remains hidden only inside tensorflow. In this case, what is the most straight-forward approach to get the thing correct and complete?

It seems that this feature is not available in the tensorlayer properly. I see that this is happening due to lots of inconsistent coding in the `convolution.py` file.

In `conv2d()` function, earlier in 1.8.3rc0 version, there was 
` new_variables = tf.get_collection(TF_GRAPHKEYS_VARIABLES, scope=vs.name)`

but, now it is changed to
` new_variables = conv2d.weights`

On the other hand, it is still retained in `deconv2d()` function as
` new_variables = tf.get_collection(TF_GRAPHKEYS_VARIABLES, scope=vs.name)`

So, for
`y = Deconv2d(x, 1, ..., act=cust_act)`,

that the parameter `alpha` does add to the `y.all_params` list in tensorlayer directly from tensorflow routine.

issue_title: Raise SPFError if DNS queries for mechnisims exceed 10
issue_body:
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7208#section-4.6.4

>   Some mechanisms and modifiers (collectively, "terms") cause DNS
>    queries at the time of evaluation, and some do not.  The following
>    terms cause DNS queries: the "include", "a", "mx", "ptr", and
>    "exists" mechanisms, and the "redirect" modifier.  SPF
>    implementations MUST limit the total number of those terms to 10
>    during SPF evaluation, to avoid unreasonable load on the DNS.  If
>    this limit is exceeded, the implementation MUST return "permerror".
>    The other terms -- the "all", "ip4", and "ip6" mechanisms, and the
>    "exp" modifier -- do not cause DNS queries at the time of SPF
>    evaluation (the "exp" modifier only causes a lookup at a later time),
>    and their use is not subject to this limit.
> 
>    When evaluating the "mx" mechanism, the number of "MX" resource
>    records queried is included in the overall limit of 10 mechanisms/
>    modifiers that cause DNS lookups as described above.  In addition to
>    that limit, the evaluation of each "MX" record MUST NOT result in
>    querying more than 10 address records -- either "A" or "AAAA"
>    resource records.  If this limit is exceeded, the "mx" mechanism MUST
>    produce a "permerror" result.

issue_title: bugfixes for util.from_n3
issue_body:
joernhees, 2012-01-25T19:07:09.000Z 
The current util.from_n3() method has a couple of bugs:
- multiline strings aren't parsed correctly:
>>> rdflib.util.from_n3('''"""multi
... line
... string"""@en''')
rdflib.term.Literal(u'""multi\nline\nstring""', lang='en')
should be:
rdflib.term.Literal(u'multi\nline\nstring', lang='en')

- datatype is truncated
>>> rdflib.util.from_n3('"foo"^^xsd:string')
rdflib.term.Literal(u'foo', datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef('sd:strin'))

- numbers and booleans aren't parsed correctly:
>>> rdflib.util.from_n3('42')
rdflib.term.BNode('42')
>>> rdflib.util.from_n3('true')
rdflib.term.BNode('true')
>>> rdflib.util.from_n3('false')
rdflib.term.BNode('false')

- given the invalid n3 syntax: '"foo"@en^^xsd:something' the language tag is chosen over the datatype (this is the opposite of the existing notation3 parser)


Attached is a mercurial patch which fixes these issues and also adds a couple of test cases to help testing.

The test cases now also include a simplistic wrapper which actually invokes the existing parsers.notation3 to make sure both result in similar rdflib terms.

Comment 1 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
Patch applied in rev 3837bf16b5fb, thanks Joern.

issue_title: Literal init with both language and datatype
issue_body:
kyleheus...@gmail.com, 2009-08-27T22:53:49.000Z 
When you initialize a Literal type with both a language and datatype, only 
datatype is set (language value is discarded and set to None)

But if you manually set a literal object's language attribute, it sticks 
regardless of whether datatype was set.

When you serialize or call the literal's n3() method, it is possible to 
output both language and datatype.

So, the question is, why can't a Literal be initialized with both langauge 
and datatype (ie. via the __new__ method)?

ex.1
>> l = Literal(u'hello',u'en-us',XSD_NS['string'])
>> l.language
None
>> l.n3()
u'"hello"^^'

ex.2
>> l = Literal(u'hello',XSD_NS['string'])
>> l.language = u'en-us'
>> l.n3()
u'"hello"@en-us^^'

Comment 1 by lindstr...@gmail.com
In RDF literal values cannot have both a language and a datatype. It's either or or neither. Thus this should 
reasonably not be changed.

(It *is* possible right now to get those strange serializations by manipulating a Literal instance though, but that 
may/should go away as well..)

I propose WontFix.


Comment 2 by eik...@gmail.com



Comment 3 by eik...@gmail.com



Comment 4 by drewpca
I propose, and I hope to write the patch tonight, that Literal raise a TypeError if 
both the args are passed in. Quietly ignoring one of the args isn't useful. Also, 
__new__ can have a docstring explaining this with a pointer to the spec, since clearly 
it's surprising people. 

In a related fix, Literal's __repr__ shouldn't ever emit datatype and language args, 
and it should not omit None args at all, just to save space.


Comment 5 by drewpca



Comment 6 by drewpca
Waiting for review: http://code.google.com/p/rdflib/source/branch?
spec=issue107&branch=/branches/literalnew-81


Comment 7 by drewpca
Merged, with a change to the rdf/xml parser, since an rdf/xml node CAN have a lang and 
datatype. (The lang is ignored, says http://www.w3.org/TR/REC-rdf-syntax/#section-
Syntax-languages)

issue_title: Allow search results filtering
issue_body:
Related to #7, it would be nice to have a clean way of filtering results on something like the object type. When all you're interested in is master releases for a certain query, a filter as follows is rather ugly:

```python
releases = [release for release in results if 'master' in release.__class__.__name__.lower()]
```

The Search object could do this in a cleaner way, for example something along the lines of

```python
search = Search('britney spears baby one more time').filter(type = 'master')
```

Needless to say, an API like this might allow more advanced filters than just the object type of the result.
